column13: In January 2017, Accomack County amended its zoning ordinance to prohibit large wind projects in agricultural districts and to require large solar projects in agricultural districts to receive special use permits from the board of zoning appeals.

column13: In November 2019, the County’s Board of Supervisors capped the number of commercial wind turbines allowed in the county at 535 turbines. At the time, there were already 532 turbines either built or under construction.

column13: In September 2024, Adams County adopted wind regulations that cap total wind turbines in the county to 225 turbines and require turbines to be set back at least 1.5 times turbine height from property lines.
column14: Ethan Hewett, Adams County board backs updated wind ordinance, KMA Land, Sept. 19, 2024, https://www.kmaland.com/news/adams-county-board-backs-updated-wind-ordinance/article_0ad479bc-767e-11ef-908e-c7fe6316baae.html.

column13: A 2018 resolution requires that wind turbines be set back 2,400 feet from neighboring dwelling units and 6,000 feet from any existing wind turbine not owned by the applicant. A 2019 resolution prohibits concentrated solar power facilities but allows photovoltaics with proper permits.
column14: Adams County, Neb., Resolution No. 2018-10-02.01 § 8.04.06(4) (Oct. 2, 2018), https://www.adamscounty.org/images/PDFS/PlanningZoning/windsystem.pdf; Adams County, Neb., Resolution No. 2019-03-05.01 § 8.13, https://www.adamscounty.org/images/PDFS/PlanningZoning/SolarRegulation2.pdf.

column13: In March 2024, the Adams County Commission passed a resolution creating a restricted area for large solar farms pursuant to S.B. 52. The commissioners cited concerns about competing interests with farmland.
column14: Commissioners pass resolution restricting large solar farms, PEOPLE’S DEFENDER, Mar. 1, 2024, https://www.peoplesdefender.com/2024/03/01/commissioners-pass-resolution-restricting-large-solar-farms/.

column13: In August 2021, the Adams Township Board unanimously voted to impose a 6-month moratorium on commercial wind and solar projects. The vote came at a time when Adams was being considered as the site for a wind farm. The president of a group opposed to that wind project— Guardians of the Keweenaw Ridge—stated that he was “ecstatic” about Adams Township imposing the moratorium.
column14: Garrett Neese, Hitting pause: Adams Township adopts a six-month wind, solar moratorium, The Daily Mining Gazette, Aug. 20, 2021, https://www.mininggazette.com/news/2021/08/hitting-pause-adams-township-adopts-six-month-wind-solar-moratorium/.

column13: The county ordinance requires that wind turbines be set back 2,000 feet from residences and 5,000 feet from schools.
column14: Allegany County, M.D, Code § 360-107 (2019), https://ecode360.com/14700310.

column13: According to the Allen County zoning ordinance, last updated in September 2019, wind energy conversion systems with any of the following three characteristics are prohibited: (a) a nameplate capacity of more than 50 kW; (b) a total height of more than 60 feet; and (c) a total swept area of more than 40 feet.
column14: Allen County, Ind., Zoning Ordinance, art. 5, ch. 6 (rev. Sept. 6, 2019), https://www.allencounty.us/images/stories/allen_county_code/T3-A5_Administration.pdf; Kevin Kilbane, More Wind Farms Likely in Indiana’s Future, but Probably Not Near Fort Wayne and Allen County, News Sentinel, Apr. 20, 2018, https://www.news-sentinel.com/news/local-news/2018/04/20/ns-series-windfarm-part-3/.

column13: On April 4, 2022, the Allen County Commission unanimously passed a resolution pursuant to S.B. 52 “declaring portions of the unincorporated areas of Allen County, Ohio to be restricted areas prohibiting construction of economically significant wind farms, large wind farms and large solar facilities.”
column14: Allen County, Ohio, Board of Commissioners Meeting Minutes at 3 (Apr. 4, 2022), https://commissioners.allencountyohio.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/M040422-Special.pdf; Jake Zuckerman, Ten Ohio counties ban wind, solar projects under new state law, Ohio Capital Journal, Aug. 23, 2022, https://ohiocapitaljournal.com/2022/08/23/nine-ohio-counties-ban-wind-solar-projects-under-new-state-law/

column13: In 2020, Almer Township adopted a wind ordinance requiring that commercial wind turbines be set back from property lines by a distance of 4 times the height of the highest blade tip. It further limits noise to 39 dBA at night and limits shadow flicker on neighboring properties to zero. The ordinance explicitly references agricultural preservation as well as health and safety concerns as the basis for the restrictions.
column14: Almer Charter Township, Mich., Ordinance 2020-03, as codified at Zoning Ordinance § 1522(A)(1), (F)(1), (F)(12), (F)(24) (Feb. 2022), https://drive.google.com/file/d/1pvjWBx5fQqVOnTaYL8I0lvDgARd_At-o/view.

column13: In March 2024, the Amherst County Board of Supervisors unanimously passed a revised zoning ordinance that establishes a cap of 50 acres under panel for utility-scale solar facilities. It also prohibits the siting of new solar projects within 1 mile of existing solar projects.
column14: Amherst County, Virginia, Ordinance No. 2024-1 (March 19, 2024), https://www.countyofamherst.com/egov/documents/1712238866_86414.pdf; Justin Faulconer, Amherst County revises zoning rules on utility solar farms, 50-acre cap now in place, AMHERST NEW ERA-PROGRESS, March 23, 2024, https://newsadvance.com/news/community/new-era-progress/amherst-county-revises-zoning-rules-on-utility-solar-farms-50-acre-cap-now-in-place/article_649ff096-e7bf-11ee-b1e9-9f3cb1fb6724.html.

column13: Arcadia Township’s 2019 zoning ordinance for wind energy limits noise to 40 dBa and establishes setbacks from property lines, occupied buildings, and rights-of-way of 1,640 feet for turbines less than 500 feet and 3.28 times turbine max blade tip height for turbines greater than 500 feet.
column14: Arcadia Township, Michigan, Article XI (General Provisions), https://www.arcadiatownship.org/images/Arcadia-Zoning-Ordinance/Arcadia%20Zoning%20Ordinance%20100%2035-61-ARTICLE%20XI.pdf.

column13: Ashe County limits the height of large wind turbines to 199 feet.
column14: Ashe County, N.C., Code of Ordinances § 163.21, https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/ashecounty/latest/ashecounty_nc/0-0-0-3857.

column13: On February 21, 2023, the Atlantic County Board of County Commissioners approved a resolution calling on state and federal officials to impose a moratorium on offshore wind development until the cause of recent whale deaths could be determined.  The resolution is nonbinding.
column14: Nanette LoBiondo Galloway, Atlantic County Commissioners approve offshore wind moratorium resolution, Down Beach, Feb. 22, 2023, https://www.downbeach.com/2023/02/22/atlantic-county-commissioners-approve-offshore-wind-moratorium-resolution/.

column13: On April 26, 2022, the Auglaize County Commission unanimously passed a resolution pursuant to S.B. 52 designating all unincorporated territories of the county as a restricted area for wind farms of at least 5 MW (i.e., “economically significantly wind farms”) and solar projects of at least 50 MW.
column14: Auglaize County, Ohio, Resolution No. 22-208 (Apr. 26, 2022), https://www.documentcloud.org/documents/22160213-auglaize-co-resolution-22-208-with-map-re-wind-exclusion-zones; see also Jake Zuckerman, Ten Ohio counties ban wind, solar projects under new state law, Ohio Capital Journal, Aug. 23, 2022, https://ohiocapitaljournal.com/2022/08/23/nine-ohio-counties-ban-wind-solar-projects-under-new-state-law/.

column13: In July 2021, the Augusta County Board of Supervisors updated the Utilities section of the County’s Comprehensive Plan to establish policies that would limit the size of industrial solar facilities. The update provided that solar farms are strongly discouraged from using more than 200 acres of land and that they should not be built in Urban Service and Community Development areas. In December 2023, Augusta County prohibited new solar projects in urban service and community development areas; at the same time, Augusta mandated that new solar projects must be located at least 2 miles from existing solar projects.
column14: Augusta County, Va., Comprehensive Plan Solar Update, July 28, 2021, https://www.co.augusta.va.us/home/showpublisheddocument/16740/637631647962170000; Tara Todd, Augusta County Supervisors pass solar restrictions despite pleas from farmland owners, WVIR, July 28, 2021, https://www.nbc29.com/2021/07/29/augusta-county-supervisors-pass-solar-restrictions-despite-pleas-farmland-owners/; Bob Stuart, Augusta County tightens ordinance on solar projects, News Virginian, Dec. 18, 2023, https://newsvirginian.com/news/local/government-politics/augusta-county-tightens-ordinance-on-solar-projects/article_37e0ef36-9d7f-11ee-b99e-4797bc93aeba.html.

column13: In March 2022, August Township adopted a 6-month moratorium on large solar energy systems, subject to renewal.
column14: Charter Township of Augusta, Mich., Resolution No. 22-08 (Mar. 22, 2022), https://augustatownship.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/03/22-08-Resolution-for-Moratorium-Large-Scale-Solar-Projects-Final-Signed.pdf.

column13: Wind energy systems with a capacity of 50 kW or greater are categorically prohibited. Any wind energy system located near a scenic byway or corridor must not cause an adverse visual impact.
column14: Baldwin County, Ala., Zoning Ordinance §§ 13.13.4, 13.13.6(j) (as amended Oct. 18, 2022), https://baldwincountyal.gov/departments/planning-zoning/ordinances-and-regulations.

column13: An ordinance adopted on June 16, 2020 requires that solar farms be set back at least 300 feet from dwelling units and at least 300 feet from property lines under most circumstances, unless owners of adjacent parcels agree to a smaller setback.
column14: Baldwin County, Ga., Code of Ordinances § 17-23(a) (adopted June 16, 2020), https://library.municode.com/ga/baldwin_county/codes/code_of_ordinances?nodeId=PTIICOOR_CH17SOENDE.

column13: In October 2023, the Bannock County Board of Commissioners enacted a 180-day moratorium on permits for large-scale energy projects. Then, in March 2024, the board enacted an ordinance that bans solar farms capable of producing 10 MW or more of energy, storing 10 MW or more of energy, or which encompass 100 or more acres of land. The ordinance also categorically bans commercial-scale wind turbines and limits non-commercial wind turbines to two turbines per lot.
column14: What’s happening with solar farms in Bannock County, Bannock County, https://www.bannockcounty.us/solar/ (last visited Oct. 1, 2024); Logan Ramsey, Bannock County Commission votes to ban large-scale solar and wind projects after packed public hearing, East Idaho News, March 26, 2024, https://www.eastidahonews.com/2024/03/bannock-county-commissioners-vote-to-ban-large-scale-solar-and-wind-projects-after-packed-public-hearing/; Bannock County, Idaho, Proposed Amendments to Bannock County Zoning Ordinance 1998-1 (Feb. 2024), https://www.bannockcounty.us/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Solar-Ordinance-Draft-Proposal-6.pdf.

column13: In May 2023, the Barton County Commission voted to require any solar farm to be 6 miles away from Cheyenne Bottoms—a wetland complex with significant wildlife—and placed a moratorium on commercial solar projects until the end of the year. In March 2024, the Commission approved an amendment to the zoning regulations, updating the zoning map of Barton County to reflect “an addition of a ‘no build’ zone around Cheyenne Bottoms as it pertains to the development of ‘Large Scale Non-Commercial Solar Energy Conversion Systems.
column14: Barton County, Kan., Resolution 2023-12 (Temporary Moratorium on Commercial Solar Energy Project Development) (adopted May 17, 2023), https://www.bartoncounty.org/vimages/shared/vnews/stories/516eb52b3853d/Resolution%202023-12%20Temporary%20Moratorium%20on%20Commercial%20Solar%20Energy%20Project%20Development%20-%20Adopted%20-%2005%2017%202023.pdf; Jackson Overstreet, ‘If there is a sacred place in Kansas, this is it’: Barton County Commission puts in temporary ban on solar farms, Kake, May 10, 2023, https://www.kake.com/story/48889571/if-there-is-a-sacred-place-in-kansas-this-is-it-barton-county-commission-puts-in-temporary-ban-on-solar-farms; Allison Kite, Kansas solar development sparks emotional debate over location near Cheyenne Bottoms, Kansas Reflector, Mar. 31, 2024, https://kansasreflector.com/2024/03/31/kansas-solar-development-sparks-emotional-debate-over-location-near-cheyenne-bottoms; Barton County, Kan., Resolution No. 2024-12 (adopted Apr. 3, 2024), https://www.bartoncounty.org/vimages/shared/vnews/stories/65aeb550022f2/2024-12%20Official%20Zoning%20Map%20Resolution%20Only.pdf.

column13: The Batavia Township Board approved a restrictive wind ordinance in September 2020 that limits the height of wind turbines to 330 feet. The restrictions were promoted aggressively on Facebook by a group called Concerned Citizens of Branch County in response to efforts by DTE Energy to develop a wind farm in the area. DTE Energy put the wind farm on hold in August 2020, after the neighboring townships of Matteson and Sherwood adopted their own restrictions on wind energy and while the Batavia restrictions were still pending.
column14: Jim Measel, Batavia Becomes Latest Township to Limit Wind Turbines, WTVB, Sept. 3, 2020, https://wtvbam.com/2020/09/03/batavia-becomes-latest-township-to-limit-wind-turbines/1054859/; Don Reid, DTE Puts Branch Wind Farm on Hold, The Daily Reporter, Aug. 6, 2020, https://www.thedailyreporter.com/story/business/energy-resource/2020/08/06/dte-puts-branch-wind-farm-on-hold/114413196.

column13: In May 2018, Beaver Township adopted restrictions on wind energy amidst opposition to DTE Energy’s plans to develop a wind farm in the area. DTE Energy argued that the noise limit was “so low that it basically would not allow any turbine in any 1-mile by 1-mile section.” As of October 2022, the township’s zoning ordinance requires that wind turbines be set back at least 1,760 feet from property lines or 4 times tower height, whichever is greater, and limits shadow flicker to zero; it also limits the height of commercial solar energy systems to 12 feet and requires that they be set back 500 feet from property lines.
column14: Isis Simpson-Mersha, DTE says township ordinance makes wind farm project ‘impossible’, M Live Michigan, May 16, 2018, https://www.mlive.com/news/bay-city/2018/05/township_makes_it_impossible_f.html; Beaver Township, Mich., Zoning Ordinance §§ 3.24(4)(b), 3.24(4)(c), 3.25(d)(2), 3.25(d)(7), 3.25(d)(11) (Oct. 26, 2022), https://storage.googleapis.com/wzukusers/user-25065735/documents/3d57f0d79e60488aab2185a88cb343c4/Zoning%20Ordinance%202019-02%20Amended%2010-26-22.pdf

column13: As of September 2021, Bedford Township only allowed solar development in industrial-zoned districts, excluding it from agricultural districts.

column13: Under a 2012 ordinance, commercial wind turbines must be set back at least 3 times the tower height from the exterior line of the project unless an agreement with affected property owners is reached. In all instances, the setback must be at least 1.5 times the tower height. Further, commercial wind turbines must be set back an additional 1 mile from “all platted Town sites and/or incorporated cities.”

column13: Boone County banned wind farms in or around 2009 after wind energy developers approached the county about potential development opportunities. In April 2024, the Boone County Board of Commissioners adopted a new 2-year moratorium on large-scale solar, wind, and storage projects.

column13: In August 2022, the Brown County Board of Commissioners approved a resolution under S.B. 52 establishing all unincorporated areas of the county as a restricted area in which the construction of large solar facilities and large or economically-significant wind farms are prohibited.

column13: In August 2021, the Brunswick County Board of Commissioners approved a resolution opposing wind farms within 24 nautical miles of the coast. The towns of Ocean Beach Isle and Sunset Beach and the Village of Bald Head Island, all within Brunswick County, passed similar resolutions.   The resolutions are nonbinding.
column14: Preston Lennon, Prospect of visible ocean wind farms unites Brunswick towns in opposition, Port City Daily, Aug. 3, 2021, https://portcitydaily.com/local-news/2021/08/03/prospect-of-viewable-wind-farms-unites-brunswick-towns-in-opposition; Village of Bald Head Island, Council Meeting Agenda Item: Request to Adopt Resolution in Opposition to Issuance of Wind Energy Leases within 24 Nautical Miles of North Carolina’s Shores, May 21, 2021.

column13: Brunswick County’s zoning ordinance limits the cumulative acreage of solar development in the county to 8,600 acres. In March 2023, the Brunswick County Board of Supervisors amended the solar ordinance to clarify that this acreage refers to the land under PV panel coverage, not the total acreage of the solar farm parcels.
column14: Sylvia Allen, Supervisors amend solar ordinance, Brunswick Times-Gazette, March 28, 2023, https://www.brunswicktimes-gazette.com/article_4f7dc64c-cca5-11ed-a9e4-67050174dff7.html; Brunswick County, Va., Code § 23-406(b), https://library.municode.com/va/brunswick_county/codes/code_of_ordinances?nodeId=CO_APXBZO_ART23SOFA_S23-406DEST.

column13: In March 2020, the Buchanan County Commission banned commercial wind turbines, citing property devaluation, noise, and effects on quality of life. In adopting the ban, the commission also noted that “[n]ot a single member of the public testified at the public hearings in favor of allowing commercial wind-energy projects.”
column14: Clayton Anderson, County commissioners ban commercial wind energy, St. Joseph News-Press, Mar. 12, 2020, https://www.newspressnow.com/news/local_news/county-commissioners-ban-commercial-wind-energy/article_f75a6494-6473-11ea-9dcc-a3e7fbffb265.html.

column13: In December 2023, Buckingham County adopted a solar ordinance that limits cumulative solar development to 1,600 MW or 7,500 acres.
column14: Rachel Austin, What does the new Buckinham County solar policy look like?, Farmville Herald, Dec. 19, 2023, https://www.farmvilleherald.com/2023/12/what-does-the-new-buckingham-county-solar-policy-look-like/.

column13: In March 2023, the Buffalo County Commissioners adopted new setback requirements for wind turbines that require distances of: 2 miles from any burial site or the Platte River; 3 miles from any agriculture residential zoned property; 3 miles from property lines of nonparticipants; 3 miles from any church, hospital, pool, or park; 5 miles from any village or city; 5 miles from any wildlife preservation area.
column14: Michael Shively, Buffalo County sets new distancing requirements for possible wind farms, News Channel Nebraska Central, Mar. 22, 2023, https://central.newschannelnebraska.com/story/48594025/buffalo-county-sets-new-distancing-requirements-for-possible-wind-farms.

column13: In February 2018, the Burnside Township Board of Trustees passed multiple amendments further restricting wind energy in their town. The amendments limited noise to 45 dBa and established setbacks of 1,640 or 3.5 times turbine max blade tip height from inhabited structures and adjacent non-participating property lines. Representatives of DTE Energy, which was considering a wind energy project in Burnside, stated that the amendments would effectively ban wind turbines in Burnside.
column14: Andrew Dietderich, Burnside officials clamp down on wind turbines, The County Press, Feb. 28, 2018, https://thecountypress.mihomepaper.com/articles/burnside-township-officials-clamp-down-on-wind-turbines/.

column13: In December 2024, the Burt County Board of Supervisors adopted new zoning regulations that require commercial wind turbines to be set back 3 miles from any towns or villages, 1 mile from property lines, and 0.5 miles from any occupied dwelling. The ordinance further limits the number of turbines in the county to a cumulative total of 20 turbines, prohibits shadow flicker on roads, and provides that noise shall not exceed 40 dBA from any dwelling on a nonparticipating property, except during periods of severe weather.
column14: Burt County, Neb., Zoning Regulations 6.03(D)(3)-(4) (updated Dec. 27, 2024), https://burtcountyne.gov/planning-and-zoning/forms-permits-and-regulations/ (last visited May 22, 2025);see also Nebraska wind project runs into opposition by residents, OK Energy Today, Feb. 24, 2020, http://www.okenergytoday.com/2020/02/nebraska-wind-project-runs-into-opposition-by-residents.

column13: Last amended in November 2022, Butler County’s zoning ordinance for unincorporated areas includes a number of restrictions on wind development. Commercial wind turbines must be set back 1,640 feet from participating property lines and 1,800 feet from non-participating property lines. Commercial wind turbines must also remain below 40 dBa.
column14: Butler County, Iowa, Ordinance Title VI Number 28 (November 1, 2022), https://butlercounty.iowa.gov/images/Butler_County_Zoning_Ordinance_No_28_11-1-22.pdf.

column13: Butler County, Ohio, Board of Commissioners Meeting Minutes at 3 (June 23, 2022), http://butlercountyoh.iqm2.com/Citizens/FileOpen.aspx?Type=15&ID=1747&Inline=True; see also Jake Zuckerman, Ten Ohio counties ban wind, solar projects under new state law, Ohio Capital Journal, Aug. 23, 2022, https://ohiocapitaljournal.com/2022/08/23/nine-ohio-counties-ban-wind-solar-projects-under-new-state-law/.

column13: In March 2023, the Calhoun County Council adopted a 6-month moratorium on the development of solar farms outside of industrial areas in unincorporated areas of the county. The moratorium did not apply to previously approved solar projects.  n October 2023, the county approved a solar ordinance that established 500-foot setbacks from town and subdivision boundaries.
column14: Gene Zaleski, Calhoun County Council: Solar farm freeze finalized, Times and Democrat, Mar. 29, 2023, https://thetandd.com/news/local/calhoun-county-council-solar-project-moratorium-considered/article_e3c372ed-652f-5844-a5f6-9592fdbe18c4.html; Caleb Bozard, Calhoun County Council: New solar farm rules advance, Times and Democrat, Sept. 20, 2023, https://thetandd.com/news/local/calhoun-county-council-new-solar-farm-rules-advance/article_1e91b54a-5407-11ee-8aca-df79a7160fab.html; Caleb Bozard, Calhoun County Council: Solar farm rules finalized, Times and Democrat, Oct. 9, 2023, https://thetandd.com/news/local/calhoun-county-council-solar-farm-rules-finalized/article_8ab185ba-62d1-11ee-a31e-c75de3b6928f.html.

column13: On March 9, 2023, Carroll County adopted a 6-month moratorium on applications for community solar farms on land currently zoned for agricultural purposes. In December 2024, the Carroll County Board of Commissioners amended its land use ordinance to increase restrictions on commercial solar projects. Under the new ordinance, ground-mounted solar systems in residential and commercial districts cannot exceed 480 square feet, and projects must be set back at least 400 feet from any public transportation corridors. In addition, the height of ground-mounted systems cannot exceed 15 feet, and facilities must include a 12-foot-tall vegetative buffer along all property lines.

column13: The City of Waltham’s zoning code effectively limited large-scale solar energy facilities to industrial zones. A developer who owned land in Waltham and a neighboring town sought permission from the city building inspector to build an access road on the land the developer owned in Waltham to a proposed 1-MW solar facility in a neighboring town. The building inspector denied permission on the basis of local zoning restrictions. On June 4, 2022, the Supreme Judicial Court struck down the restrictions, finding that “[a]n outright ban of large-scale solar energy systems in all but one to two percent of a municipality’s land area . . . restricts rather than promotes the legislative goal of promoting solar energy” and that, “in the absence of a reasonable basis grounded in public health, safety, or welfare, such a prohibition is impermissible.”  Note that this is included as a pre-March 2022 development because the relevant restriction was in place before March 2022.

column14: New regulations for future solar farms in Clark County, Indiana, WHAS11, Oct. 2024, https://www.whas11.com/article/news/local/indiana/clark-county-indiana-new-solar-farm-regulations-passed/417-abdf6d43-9de5-4328-a082-4e15e61f65dd; Clark County, Ind., Unified Development Ordinance (amended Nov. 21, 2024), https://www.co.clark.in.us/images/2024/plancommission/Clark-County-UDO.pdf.

column13: As of January 2024, Clarke County’s zoning ordinance requires that all solar energy facilities, except for those delivering behind-the-meter power, be located within 1 mile of two specified electrical substations in the county. At the time of adoption, one solar project had already been built adjacent to one of the substations, and another project had already been proposed adjacent to the other substation.

column13: Claybanks Township’s 2019 zoning ordinance establishes the following restrictions for wind turbines: setbacks of 1,500 feet from the nearest inhabited structure on a participating property and 3,000 feet from the nearest non-participating property line; a turbine max blade tip height limitation of 500 feet; and a noise limit of 40 dBa at non-participating properties. The ordinance limits solar farms to marginal land that would not be used as cropland in Agricultural Preservation and Rural Preservation Districts.
column14: Claybanks Township, Michigan, Zoning Ordinance (Aug. 2019), https://www.claybankstownship.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/08/Claybanks-Twp.-Ordinace-Book.pdf.

column11: Batavia, Jackson, Monroe, Stonelick, Tate, Washington, and Williamsburg Townships
column13: In June 2023, the Clermont County Commissioners unanimously voted to prohibit large solar and wind projects in seven townships (Batavia, Jackson, Monroe, Stonelick, Tate, Washington, and Williamsburg). Two other townships (Goshen and Ohio) likewise voted to ban large wind and solar projects but did so too late to be addressed at that meeting. After a two-hour long hearing, the three county commissioners stated “that their opinions on the potential economic benefit of solar didn’t matter because they would vote the way their residents wanted them to: in favor of the ban.”
column14: Paula Christian, Clermont County bans large solar and wind farms in seven townships: ‘We like out county the way it is’, WCPO-TV, June 21, 2023, https://www.wcpo.com/news/local-news/i-team/clermont-bans-large-solar-and-wind-farms-in-seven-townships-we-like-our-county-the-way-it-is.

column13: On September 24, 2024, Clinton County’s Planning and Zoning Commission issued a three-month moratorium on commercial wind farm applications as they revised the county’s 2008 wind ordinance. This moratorium was extended in December 2024 and will run through June 2025. A community opposition group, Clinton & Jackson County Iowa Residents Against Wind Turbines, has collected over 700 signatures for a petition against the project and has been active in public hearings regarding the proposed ordinance. [Note: As of January 2025, the proposed ordinance from the County Commission leaves little land available for commercial wind. The required setbacks for wind would be 5 miles in either direction from byway roads, 1,500 feet from property lines, and two miles from any incorporated city. A proposed wind project from NextEra Energy, which would have a capacity of 250 MW, would most likely be held back if the county supervisors approved the commission’s updated ordinance.]
column14: Jenna Blount, County Expands Wind Turbine Moratorium to Related Equipment, Clinton Herald, May 2, 2025, https://www.clintonherald.com/news/county-expands-wind-turbine-moratorium-to-related-equipment/article_efee740e-90b8-11ef-8e2e-0fa3d5fd3294.html; Jenna Blount, Wind Turbine Moratorium Extensions on Clinton County Supervisors’ Agenda, Clinton Herald, May 3, 2025, https://www.clintonherald.com/news/local_news/wind-turbine-moratorium-extensions-on-clinton-county-supervisors-agenda/article_2aa7a806-b821-11ef-817e-1baff0db5078.html; Clinton County, Iowa Residents for Stronger Wind Ordinances, Change.org, https://www.change.org/p/clinton-county-iowa-residents-for-stronger-wind-ordinances.

column13: In May 2023, the Clinton County Board of Commissioners established a 1-year moratorium on wind power plants and large-scale solar energy facilities. In April 2024, the Clinton County Board passed multiple amendments restricting utility-scale wind energy, including: a minimum setback from non-participating properties of 1,600 feet or 4 times turbine height, whichever is greater; a maximum turbine height of 450 feet; no shadow flicker on non-participating properties; and a noise limit of 45 dBA.

column13: On March 2, 2023, the Columbiana County Board of Commissioners approved a resolution pursuant to S.B. 52 prohibiting large wind and solar farms in the unincorporated areas of four townships. In May 2023, the Board ultimately approved a ban on the construction of large solar facilities and large wind farms in an additional seven townships in the county. In August 2023, the seven remaining townships in the county moved to ban large solar facilities and large wind farms. As of October 18, the county had approved bans on large wind and solar projects in all eighteen townships in the county.

column13: In May 2022, the Crawford County Board of Commissioners passed a resolution pursuant to S.B. 52 creating a 10-year ban on wind development that effectively prevents construction of the proposed 300-MW Honey Creek Wind Project. However, in November 2022, the referendum upheld the restrictions.

column13: Small wind energy conversion systems are not permitted on vacant lots; on a site listed in the National Register of Historic Places or the California Register of Historic Resources; where the system would be visible from any coastal scenic resource area; within 100 feet of any sensitive or endangered habitat designated by the California Department of Fish and Game without written permission from that agency; or within any public right-of-way, easement, path of travel, or interior traffic circulation system.

column13: A 2012 resolution provides that no wind energy project shall be approved that is set back less than 0.5 miles from the limits of the nearest city or town or 5.5 times tower height or 1 mile from the nearest residential dwelling. It further limits noise to 40 dBa at the property line.

column13: Currituck County adopted a moratorium on new solar farms in January 2017, which remained in place until January 2019. The county’s March 2022 Unified Development Ordinance does not allow “solar arrays” or “large” wind energy facilities in any zoning district.

column13: The county zoning ordinance provides that noise from wind turbines cannot exceed 30 dBA for any period of time when measured from any dwelling, school, hospital, church, or public library existing at the time of approval. column13: In November 2022, Dawson County adopted a 1-year moratorium on wind farm applications. In May 2023, the Dawson County Board of Commissioners adopted amendments to its wind regulations that impose setbacks of 3 miles from non-participating property lines, participating dwellings, road rights-of-way, and public conservation lands. The regulations also limit noise to 40 dBa at the nearest dwelling.

column13: In DeKalb County, wind turbines must be set back 2,500 feet from property lines and noise must not exceed 40 decibels. column13: In 2018, DeKalb County passed an ordinance that requires wind turbines to be set back from property lines by a distance of 6 times turbine height and 3 miles from any municipality. The ordinance also requires that zero shadow flicker occur beyond property lines and that wind projects not increase background noise levels by more than 5 dBA. These restrictions were removed to achieve compliance with Public Act 102-1123 (2023).

column13: On February 22, 2022, the Delaware County Commissioners approved amendments to the County’s solar ordinance, including increasing the setback from neighboring property lines to 200 feet and classifying solar as a “special use” subject to public hearings and additional approvals. The County Commissioners also enacted a 1-year moratorium on solar farm development; in August 2023, the Commissioners extended this moratorium through the end of 2023. The amendments and moratorium followed intense local opposition to the proposed Meadow Forge solar project.

column13: In September 2022, the Delta County Board of County Commissioners imposed a 6-month moratorium on applications for commercial-scale renewable energy facilities. The moratorium was later extended, and it was still in effect as of December 2023.

column13: In response to NextEra Energy’s proposal to construct a wind farm, the Douglas County Commission issued a moratorium on wind farm development in December 2013, which lasted until April 2014. In 2022, Douglas County adopted a solar ordinance that limits cumulative solar farm acreage in the county to 1,000 acres and requires solar panels and arrays to be at least 500 feet from any existing residence. In May 2024, after a public hearing, the Commission voted to adopt new wind regulations that increased setbacks from property lines to 2,500 feet. About two-thirds of commenters at the public hearing expressed opposition to wind energy projects in the county, with some requesting a wind energy ban. Others argued that the regulations were excessive and that the setbacks would effectively prevent wind energy development. One frequent commenter was arrested on suspicion of interfering with the conduct of public business
column14: Mackenzie Clark, Douglas County Commission approves revised wind energy regulations, The Lawrence Times, May 1, 2024, https://lawrencekstimes.com/2024/05/01/dgcocomm-vote-wind-regs/; Kansas Wind Energy Information Network, Proposed Kansas Wind Projects, http://www.kansasenergy.org/KS_wind_projects_case.htm (last visited Dec. 21, 2020); Douglas County, Kansas, Resolution No. 22-16 (May 4, 2022), https://www.jocogov.org/sites/default/files/files/2022-05/Douglas%20County%20Solar%20Regulations%20-%20FINAL.pdf.

column13: Douglass Township’s August 2023 solar ordinance establishes setbacks for industrial-scale solar of 500 feet from lot lines, 2,640 feet from wetlands, and 1 mile from lakes and the Flat River. The township’s June 2023 wind ordinance establishes a setback of from non-participating property lines of the greater of 5 times turbine height and 1,640 feet, along with a setback of 1,640 feet from inhabited structures on participating property. Noise on non-participating parcels is limited to between 30 dBa and 40 dBa, depending on time of day. Max blade tip height is limited to 350 feet, and shadow flicker on roads and non-participating parcels is prohibited.

column13: In April 2016, the Ellington Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting to vote on whether to enact a wind energy moratorium. By a unanimous vote, the board agreed to a 4-month moratorium on wind turbine development. The moratorium was supported by a group called Ellington-Almer Township Concerned Citizens, and it resulted in the delay of the Tuscola III wind farm.  The Ellington Township Zoning Ordinance, as amended July 2021, requires that wind turbines be set back by a distance of 5 times turbine height from property lines of non-participating parcels (i.e., 2,500 feet for a 500-foot turbine), and 3 times turbine height from any road. The ordinance further provides that noise shall not exceed 40 dBA at any time on a nonparticipating property and prohibits any shadow flicker on neighboring properties.

column13: In 2017, Ford County imposed a moratorium on wind energy projects while it revised its ordinance. In 2018, the county board’s zoning committee recommended increasing the existing 1,000-foot setback from buildings to 2,250 feet. At a hearing in October 2018, a group called Ford County Citizens for Property Rights demanded 3,250-foot setbacks from any property lines. On September 13, 2021, the County Board adopted an amended wind ordinance that increased setback to 2,250 feet from property lines upon which a primary structure is located. The ordinance further specified that, even if neighbors agreed to waive the setback requirement, any wind tower still must be at least 1,000 feet from the nearest primary structure. In 2023, Ford County amended its zoning rules to provide that, if the existing restrictions were "judicially determined to be invalid for any reason," then the setbacks prescribed by Public Act 102-1123 (2023) would apply.

column13: In September 2015, Franklin Township implemented regulations requiring that wind turbines be set back 1,640 feet from public roads and non-participating private property lines. The regulations were drafted by a group called Bohemian Alps Wind Watchers and implemented around the same time by five other townships in Butler County. On October 5, 2015, Butler County sent a letter to the townships challenging their authority to implement such regulations and requesting that they void the regulations. Several townships responded by stating that they would not void the regulations, but Franklin was not among them.

column13: In July 2020, Gage County adopted a 3-month moratorium on new permits for wind farms. In November 2021, Gage County adopted amendments that banned commercial wind turbines within 1 mile of property lines of nonparticipating property owners, a major change from the 2019 zoning regulations, which required only that turbines be set back from property lines by a distance of 2 times turbine height. Gage County subsequently imposed an indefinite moratorium on commercial solar facilities in 2022 to halt solar development while considering new solar restrictions. On March 14, 2023, the Gage County Planning and Zoning Commission voted to recommend new solar regulations that would require setbacks of 0.5 miles from non-participating dwellings, platted subdivisions, and platted villages, and 0.75 miles from churches, schools, natural resources districts, and National Park Service sites for any solar project greater than 2 MW. In June 2023, The Gage County Board approved the solar regulations.

column13: In June 2024 the Board of Supervisors voted unanimously in favor of amendments to the county’s Comprehensive Plan and Zoning Ordinance to regulate renewable energy facilities. The amendments define utility-scale solar projects as those with a capacity of 1 MW or greater and limit the total size of any such projects to 500 acres. The amendments further require that such projects be set back at least 1 mile from any town or city boundary and at least 2 miles from any existing solar project. Further, any solar projects interconnecting to the grid must be located within 2 miles of transmission line corridors.

column13: As of April 2019, twelve of the fifteen towns within Henry County had passed resolutions establishing buffer zones where no wind projects could be built. These buffers extend approximately 4 miles around each town. The twelve towns included Cadiz, Sulphur Springs, Kennard, Blountsville, Greensboro, Lewisville, Mount Summit, Springport, Straughn, and Middletown. In May 2019, Indiana passed a law limiting municipalities’ authority to enact such buffer zones going forward and voiding any such ordinances or resolution adopted after December 31, 2018. The law did not void any ordinances or resolutions adopted before January 1, 2019. Eleven of the Henry County town resolutions were grandfathered in under the law, but the town of Middletown’s was not.

One commissioner expressed concerns about competing land uses between wind and cattle grazing, while noting that he did not want to restrict economic development. In August 2020, the Hughes County Commission amended its zoning ordinance to require a setback of 2,640 feet (0.5 miles) or 4.9 times the height of the tower. The setback may be reduced to 1,400 feet with a waiver from the landowner.

The maximum height of turbines is 350 feet by default but may be increased to 400 feet if the applicant can show that the additional height will result in increased efficiency. In no case, however, can turbine height exceed 400 feet.

column13: In March 2022, the Kent County Levy Court imposed a moratorium on new solar projects in response to pushback after allowing solar projects to be sited on agricultural lands. In September 2022, the Levy Court amended the County ordinance to prohibit new solar farms larger than 50 acres on agricultural land, thereby reducing the potential available land from 75,000 acres to 1,500 acres, a reduction of approximately 98%. In December 2022, the Kent County Levy Court voted to impose a moratorium on utility-scale wind farms in anticipation of a future decision to prohibit wind facilities from agricultural land.

column13: In early 2017, the Kittitas County Commissioners established a temporary moratorium on applications for major solar energy projects. In July of 2017, Kittitas County Commissioners extended the moratorium by another six months and created a Citizen Advisory Committee to gather information in order to create informed regulations for the county. The moratorium was rescinded in October 2018 with the adoption of a new ordinance. The moratorium was apparently motivated by opposition to the proposed Columbia Solar Project.

column13: On January 10, 2023, the Klickitat County Commissioners adopted a 6-month moratorium on solar projects greater than 1 acre in the Goldendale and Centerville valleys. An ordinance adopted August 17, 2010 requires (a) that wind turbines be set back 1,600 feet from existing residential structures and (b) that solar panels be set back by “a minimum of five hundred [500] to one thousand five hundred [1,500] feet from existing residential structures,” depending on aesthetic impacts, geography, and the project size.

column13: In May 2024, the Knox County Board of Supervisors voted 5-1 to change setback rules for wind turbines from 2,000 feet to 6,600 feet from nonparticipating dwellings. A wind energy company, which had been working to develop a 600-MW project in the county, filed a lawsuit against the county alleging that the zoning rule changes were made “with the express purpose of regulating” the wind energy company and its proposed wind project. On May 7, 2025, the Planning Commission voted to recommend a ban on commercial wind and solar development in the county. On June 11, 2025, the County Board of Supervisors voted to adopt the Planning Commission’s recommendation to ban any new commercial wind or solar projects. See entry on North Fork Wind (Knox County) for more information.

Hazelton, Ban on Wind and solar divides Knox county residents, NEWS CHANNEL NEBRASKA, May 7, 2025, https://northeast.newschannelnebraska.com/story/52756053/ban-on-wind-and-solar-divides-knox-county-residents; Mark Mahone, Knox County OKs Ban on Future Wind, Solar Projects, YANKTON DAILY, Jun. 12, 2025, https://www.yankton.net/community/article_26c418ff-ca3c-49fd-b7ab-8ac12d779d70.html; Wind energy company, residents sue Knox County over wind farm rule changes, Siouxland Proud, 2024, https://www.siouxlandproud.com/news/local-news/wind-energy-company-residents-sue-knox-county-over-wind-farm-rule-changes/.

column13: In January 2019, Kosciusko County adopted a 60-day moratorium on permits for large-scale solar projects, which was extended by 90 days in March 2019.  Kosciusko’s zoning ordinance, last updated in January 2022, only allows commercial solar as an exceptional use in heavy industrial districts and disallows commercial solar in all other districts. Kosciusko County requires wind turbine setbacks of at least 3,960 feet or 6.5 times the turbine height from property lines. It also limits turbine noise to 32 dBA, requires zero shadow flicker effects on neighboring homes, and limits construction to dedicated industrial zones.

column13: In 2015, Lancaster County adopted updates to its zoning code to require that wind turbines maintain noise limits of 40 dBa in the day and 37 dBa at night at non-participating dwellings. The county defined turbine height as hub height plus rotor radius. In 2019, Lancaster County once again updated its wind energy regulations to require a 1-mile setback from non-participating dwellings.

column13: On September 19, 2022, the LaSalle Township Board voted to adopt an ordinance that prohibited large-scale solar energy systems from land zoned for agricultural purposes and required 500-foot setbacks from property lines. Under the new ordinance, large-scale solar energy projects are only permitted on approximately 89 acres out of 17,000 acres in the district. The proponents of the restrictions organized against solar energy development on a Facebook group called “Stop Solar! Save the Farms!” On November 29, 2024, in response to Public Act 233, LaSalle Township amended its ordinance to create an overlay district for renewable energy project, which was limited to about 1,100 acres total, before taking into account any setbacks; the new ordinance reduced solar setbacks for projects subject to P.A. 233 to 300 feet.

column13: In September 2015, Linwood Township implemented regulations requiring that wind turbines be set back 1,640 feet from public roads and non-participating private property lines. The regulations were drafted by a group called Bohemian Alps Wind Watchers and implemented around the same time by five other townships in Butler County. On October 5, 2015, Butler County sent a letter to the townships challenging their authority to implement such regulations and requesting that they void the regulations, which Linwood declined to do.

column13: In November 2023, Mahoning County commissioners unanimously approved Green Township’s ban on large wind and solar projects in unincorporated parts of the township pursuant to S.B. 52. A public hearing was held in Green Township at the end of October 2023, in which residents were largely supportive of banning wind and solar. The ban had the effect of blocking the planned 150-MW Alpin Sun solar project. In February 2024, the Mahoning County Commission approved extending the ban to 10 additional townships within the county, namely: Austintown, Beaver, Berlin, Canfield, Coitsville, Goshen, Jackson, Milton, Poland, and Springfield.

column13: In March 2020, the Matteson Township Board voted to adopt restrictions on wind energy, including by limiting turbine height to 328 feet as measured from the blade tip, despite objections from the developer of a proposed wind project that 500 feet is the typical height of commercial wind turbines in the area. The ordinance also requires a setback of 1.25 miles from property lines, which effectively excludes wind energy projects from more than 50% of the township, and requires zero shadow flicker on neighboring properties. As with Batavia Township and Sherwood Township, the restrictions were promoted aggressively on Facebook by a group called Concerned Citizens of Branch County in response to efforts by DTE Energy to develop a wind farm in the area, which was ultimately put on hold in August 2020.

In August 2020, McLean County also introduced a 2-year moratorium on solar development, purportedly to give the Coal Creek power plant an opportunity to avoid closure.

column13: In October 2023, the Mohave County Board of Supervisors cast a 3-2 vote to establish a 240-day moratorium on using energy overlay re-zoning for the development of renewable energy on private property. In the four months prior to the moratorium’s adoption, the Board of Supervisors had denied every application for energy overlay re-zoning, including those for which the county’s planning and zoning commission recommended approval. Prior to the moratorium’s adoption, a local attorney opined that it would violate Arizona Statute 11-833, which limits moratoria on rural land to situations in which a county has demonstrated that a moratorium is necessary to “prevent irrevocable public harm” from a “clear and imminent danger.” In June 2024, the Mohave County Board of Supervisors voted 3-2 in favor of allowing solar projects within the county on land zoned for “Light Industrial” use, effectively lifting the prior moratorium.

column13: In July 2022, Montgomery County passed a 1-year moratorium on wind energy development. Montgomery County approved a 180-day extension on the moratorium in June 2023. In November 2023, the county extended the moratorium indefinitely.

column13: A 2019 Zoning Ordinance renders it effectively impossible to construct wind farms in Montgomery County. The ordinance calls for setbacks from property lines or roads of either 5 times the turbine height or 2,640 feet (which may be increased to 3,200 feet at the zoning board’s discretion). It also requires setbacks of 1 mile from a town or school, a maximum of 32 dBA, and zero shadow flicker. Property values must be guaranteed for residents within 2 miles of any turbine, and commercial turbine construction is limited to industrial districts.
column14: Montgomery County, Ind., Code § 159, Article 6 (2019), https://www.montgomerycounty.in.gov/egov/documents/1619528802_25326.pdf.

column13: Montrose Township’s zoning ordinance provides that solar energy systems greater than 20 kW are allowed only in the township’s industrial zoning district. Likewise, commercial wind turbines are prohibited except in the industrial district.
column14: Montrose Township, Mich., Zoning Ordinance §§ 104-5(7), 104-15(f)(10), https://library.municode.com/mi/montrose/codes/code_of_ordinances?nodeId=PTIICOOR_CH104SOWIEN.

column13: In October 2022, the Montrose County Board of County Commissioners imposed a 6-month moratorium on the processing of applications for power generation facilities. The moratorium “does not apply to solar panels or other renewable energy components installed on private residential or commercial structures for use within the residential or commercial structure.” In April 2023, Montrose County extended the moratorium for 18 months. In December 2024, Montrose County again extended the moratorium by another 6 months.

column13: In April 2022, Montrose Township implemented a 6-month moratorium on solar and wind development.  In November 2023, Montrose Township narrowly passed a solar ordinance that requires solar projects to be set back 500 feet or more from dwellings on adjoining properties.

column13: In January 2024, the Muscatine County Board of Supervisors passed a 9-month moratorium on the issuance of special use permits for commercial wind farms, in order to provide time for the Board to consult wind farm experts and potentially promulgate revised ordinances.

column13: Nemaha County adopted a moratorium on wind energy projects in May 2019. The moratorium was most recently extended for an additional 12 months in October 2023.
column14: Greg Bebermeyer, County extends wind energy moratorium, MSC News, Oct. 4, 2023, https://www.mscnews.net/news/?nk=76435.

column13: In June 2021, Niagara County adopted a law mandating extended producer responsibility for solar panels and requiring that developers submit detailed recycling plans. As of October 23, 2023, most manufacturers remain out of compliance.

column13: In 2021, North Beaver Township passed an ordinance that prohibits solar development on agricultural land. The decision was partially in response to protests of Vesper Energy’s proposed Firefly Solar project and has hindered the project’s development.

column13: In January 2015, the Northeastern Vermont Development Association (NVDA)—one of eleven Vermont regional planning commissions—unanimously approved a resolution against the development of all industrial wind energy systems. The resolution states that “[i]t is the position of the NVDA that no further development of industrial-scale wind turbines should take place in the Northeast Kingdom.” This position was reaffirmed in NVDA’s 2018 Energy Plan and in NVDA’s June 2023 memorandum readopting that plan. NVDA’s resolution is not legally binding and is intended to serve as a “starting point” for member municipalities’ zoning process. These member municipalities are Caledonia, Essex, and Orleans counties.
column14: Northeastern Vermont Development Association, Wind Study Report, August 21, 2014 (amended January 22, 2015), http://www.nvda.net/files/Wind%20Study%20-%20Final%20Report%20-3-27-15.pdf; John Herrick, NEK development officials vote to oppose large wind projects, VT Digger, Jan. 26, 2015, https://vtdigger.org/2015/01/26/nek-development-officials-vote-oppose-large-wind-projects/; Vermont Department of Environmental Conservation, Regional Planning Commissions, https://dec.vermont.gov/waste-management/contaminated-sites/brownfields/regional-planning-commissions, last visited October 19, 2023; NVDA, Regional Plan for the Northeast Kingdom: 2015-2023, Chapter Two: Energy, Aug. 27, 2015 (amended April 26, 2018), https://www.nvda.net/nvda-regional-plan/2018FullPlan.pdf; David Snedeker, NVDA Executive Director, Memo re: Update and Readoption of Northeastern Vermont Regional Plan, June 20, 2023, https://www.nvda.net/files/AccessmentReport.pdf.

column13: Novesta Township’s wind ordinance requires turbines to be set back at least 5 times turbine height from non-participating property lines and 3 times turbine height from participating inhabited structures. The ordinance measures turbine height by the highest vertical point of the turbine’s swept rotor arc.
column14: Novesta Township, Michigan, Ordinance No. 2018, https://drive.google.com/open?id=1oXWRz9zohYoEPpeAVy9k_hcVlGyU-mSc

column13: In November 2022, the Nye County Board of County Commissioners approved a temporary 6-month moratorium on processing permits for renewable energy generation facilities. [Note: In January 2025, the moratorium expired, and a new ordinance took effect which implements a setback requirement of 1 mile for solar systems from residentially zoned property.]

column13: In September 2015, Oak Creek Township implemented regulations requiring that wind turbines be set back 1,640 feet from public roads and non-participating private property lines. The regulations were drafted by a group called Bohemian Alps Wind Watchers and implemented around the same time by five other townships in Butler County. On October 5, 2015, Butler County sent a letter to the townships challenging their authority to implement such regulations and requesting that they void the regulations, which Oak Creek declined to do.

column13: In March 2011, Ogden Township officials voted unanimously (5-0) to institute a 6-month moratorium on wind turbine construction. Ogden created a citizens advisory committee to research the effects of wind energy and alternative options for power generation.
column14: Dan Cherry, Ogden township board passes 6-month moratorium on wind turbines, Daily Telegram, Mar. 15, 2011, https://www.lenconnect.com/story/news/local/2011/03/15/ogden-township-board-passes-6/64104398007/.

column13: In March 2023, the Oglethorpe County Board of Commissioners adopted a 6-month moratorium on new solar projects. The moratorium lasted until September 2023, when the county adopted an amendment to its Zoning Ordinance of Solar Energy Systems.

column13: On October 24, 2022, the Osage County Commission unanimously adopted a measure to prohibit any new commercial wind and solar projects within the county’s borders. The county allows only small wind and solar systems of 30 kW or less for on-site power generation.
column14: Sarah Motter, Osage Co. wind farm, alternate energy development plans halted, 13 WIBW, Oct. 26, 2022, https://www.wibw.com/2022/10/26/osage-co-wind-farm-alternate-energy-development-plans-halted/; Planning Commission Memo from Russ Ewy to Osage County Planning Commission dated Oct. 6, 2022, http://ks827.cichosting.com/main/images/pdfs/Osage-County-PC-Memo-on-Alternative-Energy-Amendments-10-06-2217073.pdf.

column13: Otoe County’s zoning ordinance requires commercial wind turbines to be set back at least 1 mile from non-participating property lines, churches, and schools, and at least 2 miles from village and city limits. On January 31, 2023, the Otoe County Commissioners adopted new restrictions on wind energy: according to the meeting minutes, “turbines shall be limited to no more than two WECS per participating landowner within a one (1) mile radius of the participating property line.” In April 2024, the Otoe County Board of Commissioners enacted a moratorium on conditional use permits for commercial solar projects, which was extended in May 2024 and August 2024. In December 2024, Otoe County voted to approve zoning regulations that allow commercial solar on up to 1% of the county’s total acres. The regulations also require a 1,000-foot setback from non-participating dwellings. Several citizens speaking at the public hearing stated that they opposed seeing solar panels on land once used for agricultural purposes.

column13: In March 2022, the Page County Board of Supervisors imposed a moratorium on commercial wind applications while it considers changes to its ordinance.

column13: On June 28, 2022, Page County adopted a solar ordinance that limits utility-scale projects to 30 acres “covered by arrays of photovoltaic panels, including spaces between panels,” and other related structures. The ordinance further requires that above-ground structures, including security fences, be set back at least 300 feet from property lines with existing dwellings and that transformers be set back 600 feet.
column14: Page County, Va., Code §§ 134-7(J)-(K) (June 28, 2022), https://ecode360.com/39329837.

column13: In May 2023, voters in Palmyra Township approved a zoning amendment that imposed new restrictions on utility-scale solar projects. While the original ordinance required that utility-scale solar projects be set back 50 feet from property lines, the amended ordinance requires setbacks of 330 feet. In addition, while the original ordinance did not impose any limits on the amount of a lot that could be covered with solar panels, the amended ordinance sets a percentage maximum on lot coverage.
column14: Mary Lowe, Palmyra Township voters support zoning amendment restricting most utility-scale solar, Daily Telegram, May 3, 2023, https://www.lenconnect.com/story/news/local/2023/05/03/palmyra-township-pass-amendment-rejecting-most-utility-scale-solar/70177801007/.

column13: On June 22, 2022, the Palo Alto County Board of Supervisors adopted revisions to the county wind ordinance that set a countywide limit for wind energy production of 600 MW and required that turbines be set back from Rush Lake and Virgin Lake by 0.5 miles.
column14: Palo Alto County Approves Wind Energy Ordinance Revision, KICD, June 23, 2022, https://kicdam.com/news/170071-palo-alto-county-approves-wind-energy-ordinance-revision/.

column13: Patrick County adopted an ordinance in 2007 that prohibits any structures over 100 feet tall except for telecommunications towers and towers associated with religious buildings. The ordinance provides that “this prohibition shall be without any other exception and there shall be no variances, nor conditional nor special use permits granted from this ordinance.”
column14: Patrick County, Va., An Ordinance Entitled Prohibition of Tall Structures (Feb. 12, 2007), https://www.co.patrick.va.us/content/patrick/uploads/PDF/tall_structure_ordiance.pdf.

column13: On November 19, 2024, the Pennington County Board of Commissioners adopted a countywide 1-year moratorium on utility-scale alternative energy systems, including both solar and wind energy systems. The moratorium is set to expire on November 18, 2025.

column13: In August 2020, a moratorium on wind energy projects was extended until March 2021 with unanimous approval from the County Board. column13: The zoning ordinance, as amended in 2021, requires that commercial wind turbines be set back from property lines by a distance of 4 times blade tip height. It further limits noise to 39 dBA at night and limits shadow flicker on neighboring properties to zero. The ordinance explicitly references agricultural preservation as well as health and safety concerns as the basis for the restrictions. Pierson Township’s wind energy restrictions are virtually identical to those of Almer Township.

column13: On March 21, 2023, the Pittsylvania County Board of Supervisors approved changes to the county’s solar ordinance that will prohibit the construction of solar farms within 5 miles of any other solar farm and limit utility-scale solar projects to 2% of the total acreage of any zoning district.
column14: Diana McFarland, PittCo further restricts solar, Chatham Star Tribune, Mar. 30, 2023, https://www.chathamstartribune.com/news/article_fd43026e-ce40-11ed-a14d-c7bed4d03718.html.

column13: In 2022 and 2023, three Portage County townships (Shalersville, Nelson, and Rootstown) established temporary moratoria on large wind and solar facilities. However, as of December 31, 2024, the county has not established a restricted area pursuant to S.B. 52.

column13: In January 2021, the Posey County Commission voted to prohibit all turbines within 10 miles of the Doppler Radar Site in Owensville. A developer cancelled plans for a proposed wind project in response to the restriction. Furthermore, the Solar and Wind Ordinance for unincorporated areas of Posey County mandates that no ground-mounted solar system may be closer than 1 mile to any municipal boundary line. The Ordinance also mandates that no wind energy project may be located closer than 1 mile to any municipal boundary line or closer than 2 miles to any public, private, or parochial school property boundary line.

column13: In the fall of 2022, the Pottawatomie County Commission enacted a 1-year moratorium on utility-scale commercial solar projects. In November 2023, the County Commission voted 2-1 to extend the moratorium by 3 years.

column13: In February 2024, the Pottawattamie County Board of Supervisors adopted new wind and solar zoning restrictions. Under the new zoning ordinance, wind turbines must not exceed 412 feet in height; new solar farms must be set back at least a quarter mile (402 meters) from property lines; and new solar farms must be set back at least a half mile (804 meters) from residences.

column13: Zoning regulations require that wind turbines be set back 2,500 feet from active residential buildings, although the setback can be reduced to 1,000 feet with the written permission of all affected residents.

column13: In August 2020, Prince George County adopted a policy (but not a binding requirement) limiting the land used for solar facilities outside of heavy industrial districts to 2.74% of total acreage.

column13: In 2018, Pulaski County Commissioners banned all commercial wind development.  The Unified Development Ordinance of 2022 provides that “commercial WECS [wind energy conversion systems] shall be a prohibited use within the planning and zoning jurisdiction of Pulaski County” in the interest of “protecting and promoting the health, safety, and general welfare of the residents of Pulaski County” and “in light of concerns regarding negative impact on the value of property,” among other reasons.

column13: In September 2023, the Putnam County Board of County Commissioners adopted a resolution under S.B. 52 establishing all unincorporated areas of the county as a restricted area in which the construction of large solar facilities and large or economically-significant wind farms are prohibited.

column13: On November 18, 2024, Raisinville Township adopted an order that limited the aggregate amount of land that could be used for solar to a total of 1,000 acres across the township.

column13: In November 2022, Ralpho Township adopted a solar ordinance that allows major solar energy systems as a special exception in land zoned for agriculture and forestry, but not in residential areas. In December 2022, the owner of an amusement park who intended to build a 15-acre solar array in a residential district filed a lawsuit challenging the ordinance.

Knoebels Sons sues Ralpho Township over solar ordinance, The Daily Item, Dec. 14, 2022, https://www.dailyitem.com/news/h-h-knoebels-sons-sues-ralpho-township-over-solar-ordinance/article_88804ca8-7bd3-11ed-8354-0327d9ac355d.html; Ralpho Township, Pa., Supervisors Monthly Meeting Minutes, Nov. 8, 2022, https://www.ralphotownship.org/meeting-minutes/2021-2025/.

column13: In January 2023, Red Willow County enacted a 6-month moratorium on solar farms with the option to extend. The moratorium was enacted amidst local concerns about a proposed 54-MW solar plant north of McCook.
column14: Bruce Crosby, County Imposes Moratorium on Solar Plants, Seeks Research on Zoning, McCook Gazette, Jan. 9, 2023, https://www.mccookgazette.com/story/2979038.html.

column13: In December 2021, the Reno County Commission banned wind development in all zoned areas of the county. The vote to ban wind development in certain areas of the county came three years after residents requested that the commission impose a moratorium on wind development, ostensibly in response to news that NextEra was purchasing leases for a potential wind farm in the county. As of December 2021, a moratorium on commercial wind development was scheduled to run through March 2022. As of February 2023, the county’s zoning regulations provide that, “[i]n order to provide for an incorporated city to extend its corporate boundary and increase its tax base, no turbine shall be located within 1 mile of an incorporated city boundary.” Wind farms are permitted, however, in a designated Commercial Wind Energy Conversion Systems District in the unincorporated part of the county.
column14: John Green, Commission formally bans industrial wind in zoned areas of Reno County, The Hutchinson News, Dec. 14, 2021, https://www.hutchnews.com/story/news/2021/12/14/reno-county-commission-meeting-bans-industrial-wind-zoned-areas-county/6508762001/; Reno County, Kan., Zoning Regulations § 23-107(2)(H), https://www.renogov.org/DocumentCenter/View/9788/Zon-Article-23---Commercial-Wind-Energy-Conversion-Systems-PDF (last visited May 24, 2023); Reno County, Kan., Zoning Regulations § 24-103, https://www.renogov.org/DocumentCenter/View/9789/Zon-Article-24-CWECS-PDF (last visited May 24, 2023).

column13: In September 2015, Richardson Township implemented regulations requiring that wind turbines be set back 1,640 feet from public roads and non-participating private property lines. The regulations were drafted by a group called Bohemian Alps Wind Watchers and implemented around the same time by five other townships in Butler County. On October 5, 2015, Butler County sent a letter to the townships challenging their authority to implement such regulations and requesting that they void the regulations, which Richardson declined to do.
column14: Larry Peirce, Regulating wind turbines now a township issue, Journal Star, September 12, 2015, https://journalstar.com/news/state-and-regional/nebraska/regulating-wind-turbines-now-a-township-issue/article_3c4d3317-c7d6-54f6-bc34-c3b5560f70d4.html; Larry Peirce, Townships line up against wind development, Columbus Telegram, Sept. 23, 2015, https://columbustelegram.com/community/banner-press/news/townships-line-up-against-wind-development/article_8be33e8e-4ecd-5d6b-9049-ab7449f18244.html; Larry Peirce, Opinions differ on legality of Butler County townships’ wind turbine regulations, Journal Star, September 26, 2015, https://journalstar.com/news/state-and-regional/nebraska/opinions-differ-on-legality-of-butler-county-townships-wind-turbine-regulations/article_2c0c6b6b-7310-59fb-99b5-6aef2d0fabaf.html; Larry Peirce, Butler County tells townships to void wind turbine regulations, Journal Star, October 7, 2015, https://journalstar.com/news/state-and-regional/nebraska/butler-county-tells-townships-to-void-wind-turbine-regulations/article_af1470a4-810b-55d8-bf52-aa07275e01f1.html; Larry Peirce, Townships: Regulations to limit wind turbine development won’t be voided, Columbus Telegram, January 7, 2016, https://columbustelegram.com/community/banner-press/news/townships-regulations-to-limit-wind-turbine-development-won-t-be-voided/article_69c7f567-46dc-5423-8ff2-f33fd01e1673.html.

column13: Riga Township’s wind energy ordinance requires that on-site wind energy systems and MET towers be set back from non-participating property lines by the greater of either 2 times the height of the structure or the minimum distance necessary for noise to remain below 40 dBa.
column14: Riga Township, Michigan, Ordinance No. 32, https://mcusercontent.com/6bd0975b16a7e7a508afa9b4b/files/7ca8dff2-33ca-42db-9084-687d2e68aa01/ordinance_32_Wind_Ordinance.pdf.

column13: In July 2023, the Ringgold County Board of Supervisors approved new ordinances for wind turbines and solar. The wind ordinance requires that commercial wind turbines must be set back at least 0.5 miles (2,640 feet) from adjoining property lines, roads, and certain parks and public areas and 1 mile from state and federal parks and designated protected areas. The solar ordinance caps the total area used for utility-scale solar in the county at 400 acres, and it requires that utility-scale solar projects be set back at least 1,000 feet from occupied residences.
column14: Ringgold County, Iowa, Wind Ordinance #RC23-117 (July 24, 2023), https://cms8.revize.com/revize/ringgoldia/Wind.pdf; Ringgold County, Iowa, Solar Ordinance #RC23-117 (July 24, 2023), https://cms8.revize.com/revize/ringgoldia/Solar.pdf; Raquel Linch, Ringgold County wind and solar energy ordinances approved, KSIB Radio, July 26, 2023, https://www.ksibradio.com/ringgold-county-wind-and-solar-energy-ordinances-approved/.

column13: In August 2023, the Rio Blanco Board of County Commissioners adopted a moratorium on wind and solar projects in order to determine how to incorporate such projects into the county’s land use plan.
column14: Niki Turner, County Beat: Moratorium on new wind/solar projects approved, Herald Times, Aug. 24, 2023, https://www.theheraldtimes.com/county-beat-moratorium-on-new-wind-solar-projects-approved/rio-blanco-county/; Renewable Energy Production – Colorado, USA TODAY, https://data.usatoday.com/renewable-energy-production/colorado/08/99905/ (last visited March 11, 2024).

column13: As of its March 2023 zoning ordinance, Rockingham County caps the cumulative acreage that can be occupied by solar farms at 1,800 acres and limits individual solar projects to 50-150 acres per project.
column14: Rockingham County, Va., Code § 17-607, Solar energy facility, large (a) (as of Mar. 21, 2023), https://library.municode.com/va/rockingham_county/codes/code_of_ordinances?nodeId=CH17ZO_ARTVILAUS_S17-607SUSTCELAUS.

column13: In October 2019, Rowan County instituted a moratorium on large solar farms, set to expire in April 2020. It was extended twice. At the time of the most recent extension on October 5, 2020, it was set to expire on April 6, 2021.
column14: Ben Stansell, Planning board talks through new rules for solar farms, Salisbury Post, Oct. 27, 2020, https://www.salisburypost.com/2020/10/27/planning-board-talks-through-new-rules-for-solar-farms.

column13: Rush County limits turbines to 200 feet and imposes a setback distance of 2,640 from all property lines. The ordinance also limits shadow flicker on non-participating landowners’ properties to zero and limits noise to 32 dBa at the property line.
column14: Rush County, Ind., Zoning Ordinance §§ 6.3.7(a), (c), 6.3.8(b), (c) (2022), https://rushcounty.in.gov/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/Combine.pdf

column13: Sac County’s wind energy regulations limit shadow flicker on any human-occupied dwelling to 20 hours per year. The regulations require that wind towers be set back the greater of 3.5 times turbine height or 1,697 feet from the nearest residential dwelling, incorporated areas, public recreational areas, village residential areas, lake area development districts, urban transition districts, and event centers. The setback from a dwelling can be reduced to the height of the tower if the property owner signs a written waiver. The regulation also establishes a setback of 1.5 times turbine height from non-participating property lines, unless the property owner signs a written waiver.
column14: Sac County, Iowa, 6.14.045 Wind Energy Regulations and Definitions, https://www.saccountyiowa.gov/uploads/2/2/6/6/22665450/6_14_045_wind_energy_regulations___definititions.pdf (last visited Nov. 6, 2024).

column13: The Saline County Zoning Regulations, as amended in 2018, require that wind turbines be set back 0.5 miles from occupied structures on abutting properties. The regulations further limit noise to 40 dBA for any 10-minute average at any dwelling. In August 2020, the Saline County Planning and Zoning Board approved a 4-month moratorium on new wind farm applications. After the 6-1 vote, the Board stated that the moratorium was meant to ensure community safety and the sustainable future development of wind energy in Saline County.
column14: Saline County, Neb., Zoning Regulations §§ 619(E)(1)(e)(1), (4) (2018), https://co.saline.ne.us/pdfs/planning_zoning/zoning_regulations.pdf; H. Sterling Burnett, Nebraska County Zoning Board Places Moratorium on New Wind Farm Proposals, Heartland Daily News, Sept. 2, 2020, https://heartlanddailynews.com/2020/09/nebraska-county-zoning-board-places-moratorium-on-new-wind-farm-proposals/.

column13: In 2019, the San Bernardino County Board of Supervisors banned “utility oriented” renewable energy projects in designated “rural living” areas covering more than 1,000,000 acres of land. Residential solar panels and community solar projects are not affected.
column14: Christian Roselund, San Bernardino County bans large-scale solar, wind in some areas, PV Magazine, Mar. 1, 2019, https://pv-magazine-usa.com/2019/03/01/san-bernardino-county-bans-large-scale-solar-wind-in-some-areas/; see County of San Bernardino General Plan, Renewable Energy and Conservation Element, RE Policy 4.10, Feb. 2019, https://countywideplan.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/68/2021/02/REC-Element.pdf.

column13: In May 2023, the San Miguel County Board of Commissioners enacted a 6-month moratorium on applications for commercial energy projects (including solar projects), which was scheduled to expire in November 2024 after being extended twice. In September 2024, the Board unanimously adopted new zoning regulations on solar projects, ending the moratorium. The zoning regulations prioritize the siting of solar projects in areas that are already impacted by development. Large-scale solar projects of 30 acres or more are only permitted in certain districts and are not permitted in the Wright’s Mesa Light Industrial and Wright’s Mesa Rural Agricultural districts. Structures on solar farms are also required to be set back at least 200 feet from property lines.
column14: San Miguel County Land Use Code: 6-201(D) and 6-204(C)(II), San Miguel County, Colo., Resolution 2024-40 (Sept. 18, 2024), https://www.sanmiguelcountyco.gov/DocumentCenter/View/11891/San-Miguel-County-SOLAR-REGS-APPROVED-PDF?bidId=; Elise Schmelzer, Colorado counties’ halts on large solar energy projects are slowing progress towards state’s climate goals, The Denver Post, May 26, 2024, https://www.denverpost.com/2024/05/26/colorado-solar-energy-projects-climate-goals-local-opposition-norwood-agriculture/; Ashley Bunton, New Solar Regs Approved in San Miguel County, Telluride Daily Planet, Sept. 19, 2024, https://www.telluridenews.com/news/article_0f0bdc00-76dd-11ef-8338-73d0f0e7919f.html.

column13: In October 2016, Sand Beach Township adopted an ordinance that limits noise from turbines to 40 dBa during the day and 35 dBa at night for residents on participating properties, and 35 dBa during the day and 30 dBa at night for residents on non-participating properties. A spokesperson for NextEra energy said that these limitations would “essentially prohibit wind turbines in the township,” though Sand Beach officials disagreed.
column14: Brenda Battel, Sand Beach official: New sound ordinance doesn’t block turbines, Michigan’s Thumb, Oct. 18, 2016, https://www.michigansthumb.com/news/article/Sand-Beach-official-New-sound-ordinance-doesn-t-9978471.php; Brenda Battel, Sand Beach wind energy ordinance too quiet for developer, Michigan’s Thumb, Sept. 24, 2016, https://www.michigansthumb.com/news/article/Sand-Beach-wind-energy-ordinance-too-quiet-for-9242599.php; Brenda Battel, County planners have no comment on Sand Beach noise limits, My San Antonio, Oct. 8, 2016, https://www.mysanantonio.com/news/article/County-planners-have-no-comment-on-Sand-Beach-9947293.php.

column13: Sands Township disallows wind turbines in any zoning district—including agriculture and commercial districts—except for “Open Space” districts. Wind turbines for large wind energy systems must be set back at least 1,000 feet from non-participating properties, cannot exceed 500 feet in height, and must remain below 40 dBa in noise. The owners of large wind energy systems “must take such reasonable steps as are necessary to prevent, mitigate, and eliminate Shadow Flicker” on occupied structures on nonparticipating property. Solar farms larger than 5 acres are not allowed in agriculture zoning districts.
column14: Sands Township, Michigan, Zoning Ordinance (last amended Nov. 15, 2022), https://www.sandstownship.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/Zoning-Ordinance-Updated-November-2022.pdf.

column13: The Sanilac Township wind ordinance, with changes adopted in June 13, 2019, establishes an exclusion zone on wind facilities east of Ridge Road. It further prohibits shadow flicker on any neighboring property, limits noise to 5 dBA higher than ambient noise, and requires a setback from property lines of at least 1,320 feet or 4 times total height, whichever is greater.
column14: Sanilac Township, Mich., Wind Ordinance § 6.50(e), (g)(1), (g)(4), (g)(9) (June 13, 2019), https://sanilactownship.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/Wind-50-51-Final-copy-with-changes-adopted-June-13-2019.pdf.

column13: In 2010, Santa Clara County adopted an ordinance that prohibits solar farms from land designated as “Agriculture—Large scale”; the ordinance also prohibits solar farms from land designated as “Agriculture—Medium scale,” unless the land is deemed to be of “marginal quality for agricultural purposes.”
column14: Santa Clara County, Cal., Code of Ordinances § 4.10.345(A), https://library.municode.com/ca/santa_clara_county/codes/code_of_ordinances?nodeId=TITCCODELAUS_APXIZO_ART4SUSTRE_CH4.10SUUSRE_S4.10.345SOENCOSYOM.

column13: In September 2015, Savannah Township implemented regulations requiring that wind turbines be set back 1,640 feet from public roads and non-participating private property lines. The regulations were drafted by a group called Bohemian Alps Wind Watchers and implemented around the same time by five other townships in Butler County. On October 5, 2015, Butler County sent a letter to the townships challenging their authority to implement such regulations and requesting that they void the regulations. Several townships responded by stating that they would not void the regulations, but Savannah was not among them.
