column13: In January 2024, Scotts Bluff County passed a 6-month moratorium on new conditional use permits for renewable energy projects.
column14: R. Murphy, Scotts Bluff Co. Board approves six-month moratorium on new conditional use permits for renewable energy, KNEB, Jan. 17, 2024, https://ruralradio.com/kneb-am/news/scotts-bluff-co-board-approves-six-month-moratorium-on-new-conditional-use-permits-for-renewable-energy/.

column13: Wind energy conversion systems are “prohibited within the unincorporated portion of Sedgwick County and the City of Wichita.” In September 2023, Sedgwick Country enacted a 6-month moratorium on solar applications as it considered new solar regulations. In March 2024, the Sedgwick County Commission extended the September 2023 moratorium on new commercial solar farms until September 2024. In August 2024, the county adopted new regulations, officially ending the moratorium. The new regulations prohibit solar projects from exceeding 1,500 acres in area; prohibit solar projects from being located in or on more than 6 contiguous sections of land; and require new solar projects be set back at least 2 miles from the nearest solar project.

column13: On November 23, 2021, the Seneca County Board of Commissioners approved a resolution pursuant to S.B. 52 prohibiting wind and solar projects generating at least 50 MW from all unincorporated areas.
column14: Commissioners approve resolution restricting future wind, solar energy projects in Seneca County, TiffinOhio.Net, Nov, 24 2021, https://go.tiffinohio.net/2021/11/commissioners-approve-resolution-restricting-future-wind-solar-energy-projects-in-seneca-county

column13: In June 2024, the Shawnee County Commission voted to ban community- and commercial-scale wind energy conversion systems in the county and unanimously approved a 1-year moratorium for solar energy conversion systems.

column13: In September 2023, the Shelby County Commission approved a 180-day moratorium on commercial solar farm developments in the Conservation Agricultural zoning district of the county.
column14: Brooke Muckerman, Shelby County Commission: solar farm moratorium approved, SCSO changes discussed, MEMPHIS COMMERCIAL APPEAL, Sep. 11, 2023, https://www.commercialappeal.com/story/news/government/county/2023/09/11/shelby-county-commission-gets-new-chairwoman-moratorium-on-solar-farms-approved/70775172007; Shelby County, Tennessee, Resolution Establishing a 180-Day Moratorium on New Commercial Solar Farms, https://shelbycountytn.gov/DocumentCenter/View/41227/SOLAR_FARM_MORATORIUMDOC#:~:text=COMMISSIONERS%2C%20That%20this%20Board%20of,districts%20of%20unincorporated%20Shelby%20County.

column13: On April 25, 2023, the Shenandoah County Board of Supervisors amended the county solar ordinance to require 100% vegetative coverage around the perimeter of a facility and to limit the size of facilities to 50 acres for every mile of buffer.
column14: Alex Bridges, Shenandoah County board OKs new solar farm rules, The Northern Virginia Daily, Apr. 26, 2023, https://www.nvdaily.com/nvdaily/shenandoah-county-board-oks-new-solar-farm-rules/article_916f0142-729b-5e3d-aa6f-ba622c07aa6c.html.

column13: In December 2019, the Sherwood Township Board voted to adopt a restrictive wind ordinance (later approved by voters in August 2020) that limits turbine height to 330 feet (approximately half the height of the tallest onshore turbines) and imposes a setback from property lines equal to 500% of tower height and additional setbacks of 0.5 miles from water, 1 mile from the Village of Sherwood, and 2 miles from environmentally sensitive areas. As with Batavia Township and Matteson Township, the restrictions were promoted aggressively on Facebook by a group called Concerned Citizens of Branch County in response to efforts by DTE Energy to develop a wind farm in the area, which was ultimately put on hold in August 2020.
column14: Ken Delaney, Group Wins Victory in Slowing Down Turbines, WTVB, Dec. 6, 2019, https://wtvbam.com/2019/12/06/group-wins-victory-in-slowing-down-turbines/964242/; Don Reid, DTE Puts Branch Wind Farm on Hold, The Daily Reporter, Aug. 6, 2020, https://www.thedailyreporter.com/story/business/energy-resource/2020/08/06/dte-puts-branch-wind-farm-on-hold/114413196.

column13: In June 2018, Shiawassee adopted specific use regulations for wind farms, limiting max blade tip height for commercial wind farms to 450 feet and noise to 45 dBa, prohibiting shadow flicker, and establishing setbacks for commercial wind turbines of 350% turbine max blade tip height from non-participating property lines.
column14: Shiawassee County, Michigan, Specific Use Regulations for Wind Energy Conversion Systems (June 14, 2018), https://shiawassee.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/ZONING-ORDINANCE-AMENDMENTS-Part-2-update.pdf.

column13: On July 8, 2021, the Sidney Township board approved an ordinance that prohibited wind turbines within 3,000 feet of any road and 2.5 miles from any body of water. The ordinance also limited shadow flicker to zero, and limited turbine height to 300 feet. The restrictions made it impossible for Apex Clean Energy to build a planned wind farm.
column14: Joseph Bernstein, “Corrosive Communities”: How A Facebook Fight Over Wind Power Predicts the Future of Local Politics in America, Buzzfeed News, Dec. 17, 2021, https://www.buzzfeednews.com/article/josephbernstein/facebook-groups-wind-turbine-construction; Sidney Township, Mich., Ordinance No. 2021-01 §§ 16.08(LL)(5)(a), (b), (c), http://www.sidneymi.org/nebula.wsimg.com/f8ea6dd989ee7296e9bba416c788ce3f5791.pdf?AccessKeyId=D83E7EFEAA9A7A865547&disposition=0&alloworigin=1.

column13: In September 2022, Sioux County adopted a 1-year moratorium on commercial wind development. Sioux County extended the wind energy moratorium for another year in September 2023 and also enacted a moratorium on commercial solar projects.

column13: In September 2015, Skull Creek Township implemented regulations requiring that wind turbines be set back 1,640 feet from public roads and non-participating private property lines. The regulations were drafted by a group called Bohemian Alps Wind Watchers and implemented around the same time by five other townships in Butler County. On October 5, 2015, Butler County sent a letter to the townships challenging their authority to implement such regulations and requesting that they void the regulations. Several townships responded by stating that they would not void the regulations, but Skull Creek was not among them.

column13: In July 2024, the Smith Township Planning Commission began discussions to amend its solar ordinance. On September 4, 2024, the Commission held a special meeting to discuss the proposed revisions. On September 18, 2024, the Commission voted unanimously to submit the proposed amendments to the Board of Supervisors for approval. On November 12, 2024, the Board unanimously voted to adopt the new solar ordinance 2024-5. The amendments include: increasing the setbacks; adding Principal Solar Energy Systems (PSES) and Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) to the Conservation District (C-D) as a conditional use and removing those from the Rural Residential (A-1) District; requiring an endangered species report; requiring a decommissioning plan to be submitted during land development and updated every three years instead of every five years; requiring a decommissioning bond to be set at 125% and reviewed every three years; and requiring a review from the Historical Society.
column14: Smith Township Code of Ordinances, PART 11, Section 1103, Subsection 56, et al, https://ecode360.com/26942413#26942417; Special Meeting Minutes (Sep. 18, 2024), https://www.smithtownship.org/minutes; Smith Township Solar Energy Systems Draft Ordinance, https://irp.cdn-website.com/3c6a5956/files/uploaded/solar-ordinance-2024.pdf; Smith Township Board of Supervisors Meeting Minutes (Nov. 18, 2024), https://climate.law.columbia.edu/sites/climate.law.columbia.edu/files/content/Regular%20Meeting%20Minutes%20-%20November%202024.pdf.

column13: In September 2022, Southampton County adopted a solar ordinance that limits the cumulative acreage of solar farms in the county to 3,855 acres, or 1% of county land. Southampton also requires a 5-mile setback between any two solar farms and a 5-mile setback between a solar farm and an incorporated municipality.

column13: In May 2023, Spencer County adopted a year-long moratorium on the permitting of commercial solar projects.

column13: In March 2023, residents of the town of Albion voted at a Town Meeting to adopt a new ordinance that requires solar projects to be set back 300 feet from roads or residential buildings. The vote was in response to the Aroostook Renewable Gateway Project, which is planned to transmit power from a new large wind project in Northern Maine. Residents expressed concerns that the project would disrupt farmland and disorient bees through electric and magnetic fields.

Two of the board members had lived close to wind turbines and expressed that their families had been negatively impacted by the sound and shadow flicker. In order to go into effect, however, the amendment would require the Town Council’s final approval. As of June 2022, the Council had yet to vote on the ordinance.
column14: Jacquelyn Moorehead, Foster Planning Board votes to prohibit wind turbines, The Valley Breeze, Apr. 14, 2021, https://www.valleybreeze.com/news/foster-planning-board-votes-to-prohibit-wind-turbines/article_fe55f3e2-5989-52fb-9378-62b10a56edab.html#.YHsRSS1h3yg; Foster, Rhode Island, Planning Board Meeting Minutes, June 1, 2022, https://www.townoffoster.com/sites/g/files/vyhlif4421/f/minutes/6-1-22_pb_minutes_approved.pdf.

column13: The Town of Frankfort adopted a wind energy ordinance in May 2023 that limits noise to 35 dBA at non-participating property lines and residences and requires large wind turbines to be set back the greater of 1 mile or 10 times turbine height from facility property lines.  Frankfort is one of several towns in Clark and Marathon Counties to adopt similar ordinances.
column14: Town of Frankfort, Wisconsin, Ordinance No. 2023-0508-A (May 8, 2023), https://irp.cdn-website.com/bd9533a3/files/uploaded/Town%20of%20Frankfort%20-%20Ordinance%2020230508-A.pdf.

column13: In November 2015, the Town of Freedom enacted a wind turbine ordinance that requires all wind turbines be set back 2,500 feet from any Scenic or Special Resource. Depending on the height and megawatt capacity of the turbine, the ordinance requires it be setback either 1.5 times turbine height, 3 times turbine height, or 13 times turbine height from non-participating landowners. Turbines less than 100 feet in height and less than or equal to 10 kW in capacity must be set back at least 1.5 times turbine height; turbines between 100 and 150 feet in height and less than or equal to 10 kW in capacity must be set back at least 3 times turbine height; and turbines greater than 150 feet in height and/or greater than 10 kW in capacity must be set back at least 13 times turbine height. The ordinance measures turbine height by the highest point of any turbine blade tip. Shadow flicker is limited to ten hours per year, and depending on the time of day and height of turbine, noise is limited to between 30 and 40 dBa.
column14: Town of Freedom, Maine, Wind Turbine Ordinance (enacted November 17, 2015), https://www.freedomme.org/_files/ugd/b8ba64_202edae5794143029c03fed03d174f66.pdf.

column13: In January 2011, the Town of North Kingstown placed a 6-month moratorium, which was extended by 90 days in June 2011. The moratorium was motivated by opposition to a proposal to construct two wind turbines in the town. However, those two turbines were not subject to the moratorium. As of March 2023, wind energy systems are prohibited in the town, not including any approved prior to November 21, 2011.

column13: In May 2024, residents of Northfield voted in favor of two citizen’s petitions to place 1-year moratoriums on utility-scale solar projects and battery storage facilities. During the annual town meeting, proponents argued that the moratoriums would provide time for the town to draft bylaws regulating utility-scale solar projects and battery storage facilities. Opponents argued that the moratoriums would halt the town’s efforts toward clean energy. Some commenters noted that the moratorium might conflict with state law, as Chapter 40A, Section 3 of Massachusetts General Law states that “No zoning ordinance or bylaw shall prohibit or unreasonably regulate the installation of solar energy systems or the building of structures that facilitate the collection of solar energy, except where necessary to protect the public health, safety or welfare."

column13: In May 2023, Pelham residents voted to amend the town’s solar and energy storage bylaws. The town prohibited energy storage systems as a principal use in all zoning districts and required all large-scale solar projects (defined as installations between 1.5 and 15 acres of land) to be set back at least 500 feet from nearby streets. The town further required developers to set aside 4 times the total area of a solar installation for forestland conservation, if the project would convert any existing forestland for solar development. In December 2023, the Massachusetts Attorney General’s office sent a letter to the town rejecting portions of the amended bylaw, including the forestland conservation requirement, noting that certain bylaw provisions failed to comply with state law that prohibits municipalities from banning solar installations through zoning.

column13: In December 2022, the Town of Rotterdam adopted a 1-year moratorium on large-scale solar projects. In September 2023, Rotterdam adopted 6-month moratoria on battery energy storage facilities and wind projects. Then, in January 2024, the town extended its solar project moratorium by 6 months, and, in April 2024, the town extended its battery energy storage and wind project moratoria by 6 months as well.

column13: Local Law No. 6 of 2014 limits the cumulative capacity of major solar energy systems across the town to 16 MW.

column13: Shutesbury’s solar bylaw, adopted in May 2008 and amended in June 2021, prohibits solar plants larger than fifteen acres, among other restrictions. In January 2023, Shutesbury residents voted to adopt a revised town bylaw for solar arrays at a Special Town Meeting. The revised bylaw would have banned energy storage systems as a principal use in all zoning districts and limited large-scale solar installations (defined as installations between 1.5 and 15 acres of land) to a special use in Forest Conservation Districts, prohibiting such installations in all other districts. The Massachusetts Attorney General’s office sent a letter to Shutesbury officials disapproving of the January 2023 bylaw, partially based on a determination that the bylaw violated state law by effectively banning solar installations. The Attorney General’s office’s action resulted in the pre-2023 bylaw regaining legal effect. The pre-2023 bylaw is the subject of a lawsuit filed by developer PureSky Energy in August 2023, in which PureSky alleges that the town’s solar regulations are in violation of state law that prohibits unreasonable regulation of solar energy systems.

Weaver, Developer challenges Masschusetts town on solar zoning restrictions, PV Mag., Aug. 9, 2023, https://pv-magazine-usa.com/2023/08/09/developer-challenges-massachusetts-town-on-solar-zoning-restrictions/; Scott Merzback, Shutesbury seeks dismissal of suit filed by solar biz, Daily Hampshire Gazette, Aug. 2, 2023, https://www.gazettenet.com/Shutesbury-seeks-dismissal-of-Land-Court-lawsuit-related-to-large-scale-solar-projects-51819907; Scott Merzbach, State attorney general’s office rejects Shutesbury’s revised solar bylaws, Daily Hampshire Gazette, Aug. 2, 2023, https://www.gazettenet.com/Shutesbury-seeks-dismissal-of-Land-Court-lawsuit-related-to-large-scale-solar-projects-51819907.

column13: In January 2018, the Somerset town board unanimously passed a law that effectively banned industrial wind turbines in the town. The zoning amendments banned structures over 150 feet in height, prohibited wind turbines outside the town’s small industrial zones, prohibited wind turbines that sell electricity off-site, and required turbines to be set back as far as 1 mile from buildings. The restrictions appeared to be aimed at stopping the proposed Lighthouse Wind project.

column13: An ordinance adopted in April 2021 sets out Town “policies” that discourage siting utility-scale solar farms on agricultural land. Among the Town policies are the following statements: (a) “Do not site [solar energy systems] on Group I or II agricultural soils” and (b) “Discourage [siting of solar energy systems] in other areas used for crop production.”  The restrictions are nonbinding.

column13: In 2023, the Town of Trenton’s Select Board passed a moratorium on large-scale Solar Projects in response to a proposed 70-MW solar farm. The moratorium was extended multiple times, including a 180-day extension in September 2024. In the meantime, on May 2024, the Town of Trenton voters rejected a proposed ordinance to facilitate large-scale solar development. Opponents of solar development in the town include a group called Neighbors Against More Large-Scale Solar Development.

The ordinance prohibits new energy projects from being located on prime farmland, which it defines as farmland “which has the soil quality, growing season, and moisture supply needed to produce economically sustained high yields of crops when treated and managed according to acceptable farming methods.”

column13: In March 2023, the Union County Board of Supervisors extended an industrial wind energy moratorium until December 2023. The moratorium was again extended until its eventual expiration in July 2024, when the county adopted changes to its wind ordinance. The county’s new wind ordinance requires that wind turbines be set back the greater of 0.62 miles (3,281 feet) or 7 times turbine height from a property line.

column13: The Wells County Zoning Ordinance, last updated in November 2022, prohibits “large” wind energy conversion systems throughout the county. The ordinance defines “large” turbines as those with more than 100 KW of peak generation capacity.

column13: On October 28, 2020, the Wheeler County Commissioners voted to prohibit “Commercial/Utility Grade Wind Energy Systems.” On November 25, 2020, the county attorney informed the commissioners that their vote to completely prohibit utility-scale wind was unconstitutional and advised them to consider other restrictions. On December 9, 2020, the commissioners voted to require a 5-mile setback from any dwelling, to cap turbine height at 299 feet, and to require a distance of 0.5 miles between turbines.

column13: In January 2023, White River Township adopted a 6-month moratorium on solar projects, prompting a lawsuit from the developer of a proposed 200-MW project who had already spent $1.6 million on the project. In 2024, the township adopted an ordinance that restricted renewable energy projects to an overlay district that consists of a single 600-acre contaminated brownfield parcel.

Michigan Public Service Commission, Court of Appeals No. 373259,  Feb. 7, 2025, at 8, https://climate.law.columbia.edu/sites/climate.law.columbia.edu/files/content/Michigan%20Almer%20Charter%20Twp%20Briefs/2025.02.07%20Intervening%20Appellees'%20Brief%20on%20Appeal.pdf; Lynn Moore, Solar power developer sues township for failing to consider large project in West Michigan, M Live Michigan, Mar. 2, 2023, https://www.mlive.com/news/muskegon/2023/03/solar-power-developer-sues-township-for-failing-to-consider-large-project-in-west-michigan.html.

column13: Whitley County requires setbacks from property lines equal to 6.5 times the height of the tower or 2,640 feet, whichever is greater.
column14: Whitley County, Ind., Zoning Ordinance, ch. 5 WECS-02(E)(2), https://www.whitleygov.com/egov/documents/1439231634_83082.pdf (last visited Feb. 8, 2023); Christopher Stephens, Wind farm foe rezones to fight back, The Herald Bulletin, July 17, 2016, https://www.heraldbulletin.com/news/local_news/wind-farm-foe-rezones-to-fight-back/article_8d398421-ac87-5020-8c23-3a73eac099cd.html.

column13: Winfield Township adopted wind and solar ordinances in November 2023. The solar ordinance requires that photovoltaic and thermal panels be set back at least 300 feet from property lines and public rights-of-way, and requires that inverters and transformers be set back at least 500 feet from property lines and public rights-of-way. The wind ordinance requires that wind turbines not exceed 300 feet in height and be set back at least 1,640 feet from property lines and public rights-of-way. Noise from wind turbines cannot exceed 45 dBa, and shadow flicker is prohibited on all public rights-of-way and non-participating properties.
column14: Winfield Township, Michigan, Ordinance No. 11-9-23-3 (Nov. 9, 2023), https://winfieldtownshipmi.gov/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/Commercial-Solar-Energy-1.pdf; Winfield Township, Michigan, Ordinance No. 11-9-32-2 (Nov. 9, 2023), https://winfieldtownshipmi.gov/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/Commercial-Wind-Energy-1.pdf.

column13: On August 23, 2022, the Woodbury County Board of Supervisors adopted a new ordinance to increase the setback distance from wind turbines to occupied residences from 1,250 feet to 2,500 feet. This reduced the buildable acreage in the county from 177 to 1.7. The change is expected to kill MidAmerican Energy’s proposed Siouxland Wind Farm. On May 22, 2023, the Woodbury County Board of Supervisors again increased the setbacks from residential areas to 2 miles and imposed a 1-mile setback from conservation areas. In August 2023, Woodbury County supervisors further amended the zoning ordinance to disallow utility-scale solar energy systems in agricultural protection districts, allowing them only in general industrial districts. On October 1, 2024, the Woodbury County Board of Supervisors extended setback of wind turbines from farm homes and towns to 1 kilometer or 3,280 feet, equivalent to a setback of 5.45 times turbines height.
column14: Connor Trett, Woodbury County Board of Supervisors Adopts Amendments to Wind Turbine Ordinance, KTIV, Oct. 1, 2024, https://www.ktiv.com/2024/10/02/woodbury-county-board-supervisors-adopts-amendments-wind-turbine-ordinance/; Woodbury County, Iowa, Ordinance No. 67 (Aug. 23, 2022), https://www.woodburycountyiowa.gov/files/county_ordinances/67_amending_portions_of_ordinance_56_modifying_section_61a_737.pdf; Kendall Crawford, Woodbury County Adopts an Ordinance to Limit Wind Energy, Iowa Public Radio, Aug. 24, 2022, https://www.iowapublicradio.org/environment/2022-08-24/woodbury-county-adopts-an-ordinance-to-limit-wind-energy; Woodbury County, Iowa, Ordinance No. 72 (May 23, 2023), https://www.woodburycountyiowa.gov/files/county_ordinances/72_ordinance_amending_portions_of_woodbury_county_ordinance_56_an_amendment_to_modify_section_61a_wind_turbines_set_back_requirements_to_increase_certain_235.pdf; Jonathan Mack, Woodbury County approves wind turbine ordinance, Siouxland Proud, May 23, 2023, https://www.siouxlandproud.com/news/local-news/woodbury-county-approves-wind-turbine-ordinance/; Woodbury County, Iowa, Ordinance No. 74 (August 8, 2023), https://www.woodburycountyiowa.gov/files/county_ordinances/74_ordinance_amending_text_of_zoning_ordinance_to_amend_portions_of_the_table_of_contents_section_3034_entitled_land_use_summary_table_of_allowed_uses_in_705.pdf; Laigha Anderson, Woodbury County Supervisors Approve Solar Ordinance, Siouxland Proud, Aug. 8, 2023, https://www.siouxlandproud.com/news/local-news/woodbury-county-supervisors-approve-solar-ordinance/.

column13: Worcester County regulations provide that large wind energy conversion systems with a capacity greater than 100 kW are “not permitted in any district.”
column14: Worcester County, M.D., Code § ZS 1-344(C)(1)(c) (June 15, 2015), https://dnr.maryland.gov/pprp/Documents/WorcesterZoningDocs.pdf.

column13: In April 2021, the Worth County Board of Supervisors adopted a moratorium on wind energy development until July 2022, at the same time as the developer Invenergy was in the process of obtaining easements for a 30,000-acre wind farm. Worth County adopted new wind regulations in June 2022. These regulations established a setback requirement of the greater of 1,600 feet, 3.75 times turbine height, or manufacturer safety distances from property lines and buildings.
column14: Worth County passes commercial wind moratorium, Albert Lea Tribune, April 13, 2021, https://www.albertleatribune.com/2021/04/worth-county-passes-commercial-wind-moratorium/; Jaci Smith, Worth County passes commercial wind moratorium, Gazette Globe, June 7, 2023, https://globegazette.com/news/local/govt-and-politics/worth-county-passes-commercial-wind-moratorium/article_736d9ad5-9ca1-5741-8427-8066185da943.html; Worth County, Iowa, Ordinance 209 (June 27, 2022), https://worthcountyiowa.gov/files/county_ordinances/2022.06.27_wind_ordinance_209.pdf.

column13: In 2021, Wright County implemented a 1-year moratorium on 5-acre community solar gardens in agricultural areas. The county subsequently adopted new restrictions requiring a 1-mile separation between solar gardens.
column14: Matt McKinney, Solar vs. scenery: Scandia solar panels show a tense debate, Star Tribune, Mar. 19, 2022, https://www.startribune.com/solar-garden-beauty-in-eye-of-beholder/600157619/.

column13: On July 26, 2022, Yakima County adopted a temporary moratorium on mid- and large-scale solar projects, which was extended on February 28, 2023, for another 6 months. In August 2023, the moratorium was extended again until February 15, 2024. In January 2025, the moratorium was once again extended for another 6 months.

column13: In 2023, Rhode Island enacted a solar siting bill that increases the annual statewide cap on new Renewable Energy Growth developments from 40 MW to 300 MW per year but prohibits participating developers from developing solar farms in “core forests.” The law defines core forests as “unfragmented forest blocks of single or multiple parcels totaling two hundred fifty (250) acres or greater unbroken by development and at least twenty-five (25) yards from mapped roads.” Exceptions could be allowed for projects already underway or projects on “preferred sites” within core forests.
column14: Rhode Island 2023-S 0684A, 2023-H 5853A; Press Release, State of Rhode Island General Assembly, Sen. DiMario/Rep. Speakman solar siting bill enacted (June 26, 2023), https://www.rilegislature.gov/pressrelease/_layouts/15/ril.pressrelease.inputform/DisplayForm.aspx?List=c8baae31-3c10-431c-8dcd-9dbbe21ce3e9&ID=373842.

column13: In 2024, Florida enacted a state law that prohibits the construction or expansion of any wind energy facility (a) offshore; (b) within 1 mile of the coastline; (c) within 1 mile of the intercoastal waterways; (d) on any waters or submerged lands.
column14: Fla. Stat. § 377.708 (2024), https://www.flsenate.gov/laws/statutes/2024/377.708.

column13: In July 2021, Governor Janet Mills signed into law L.D. 1619, sponsored by Senator Mark Lawrence. With some exceptions, the law permanently banned the development of all offshore wind projects in Maine state waters but allowed the permitting of offshore wind in federal waters if the Governor’s Energy Office developed a planned research strategy for such projects by March 2023. The law also created the Offshore Wind Research Consortium (ORWC) to study the effects of offshore wind energy on the Gulf of Maine. The law was supported by Maine lobstermen, who expressed concerns about the impact of offshore wind on the state’s fishing industry.
column14: Maine P.L. 2021 c. 407; Nick Linder, Mills sign offshore wind ban amid lingering skepticism among fishermen, Maine Wire, July 11, 2021, https://www.themainewire.com/2021/07/mills-signs-offshore-wind-ban-amid-lingering-skepticism-from-fishermen/.

column13: On May 23, 2019, the Oregon Department of Land Conservation and Development adopted regulations that restrict the construction of solar facilities on certain categories of farmland. The new rules do not allow solar facilities that use, occupy, or cover more than 12 acres of prime farmland or 20 acres of other farmland unless an exception applies. The regulations effectively prohibit commercial-scale solar projects from 86% of the Willamette Valley.
column14: Sarah Stauffer Curtiss, Oregon’s DLCD Finalizes Solar Siting Rules, Stoel Rives Renewable Law Blog (July 3, 2019), https://www.lawofrenewableenergy.com/2019/07/articles/solar/oregons-dlcd-finalizes-solar-siting-rules/; Jim Rue, Rulemaking – Solar Facilities on High-Value Farmland, Jan. 10, 2019, https://www.oregon.gov/lcd/Commission/Documents/2019-01_Item_6_Solar_Final.pdf.

column13: Governor LePage signed an executive order in January 2018 that imposed a moratorium on wind energy development in certain parts of western and coastal Maine. In February 2019, Governor Mills signed an executive order to end the moratorium.
column14: Maine Exec. Order No. 2 FY. 17/18 (Jan. 24, 2018); Maine Exec. Order No. 3 FY. 19/20 (Feb. 14, 2019), https://www.maine.gov/governor/mills/sites/maine.gov.governor.mills/files/inline-files/Governor%20Mills%20Executive%20Order%203%20Signed%20Feb.%2014%2C%202019%20_0.pdf.

column13: In May 2017, the Tennessee General Assembly passed a 1-year moratorium on the construction, operation, expansion, or redevelopment of wind energy facilities in the state, with an exemption for counties and municipalities that adopted regulations for siting wind energy facilities on or before July 1, 2017. The bill also created a temporary six-member joint legislative study committee to evaluate and make recommendations related to siting wind energy facilities.
column14: Tennessee General Assembly, HB 1021/SB 1336, https://wapp.capitol.tn.gov/apps/BillInfo/default.aspx?BillNumber=HB1021&ga=110 (last visited October 19, 2023), Craig Clark, Senate vote last Thursday freezes wind farm project, For The Vista, May 11, 2017, http://vistanewspaper.com/senate-vote-last-thursday-freezes-wind-farm-project/.

column13: In July 2017, House Bill 589 / Senate Law 192 (“Competitive Energy Solutions for NC”) was signed into law. The law included a moratorium on the permitting of wind energy facilities until the end of 2018 for the purpose of studying the impact of future wind energy development on military operations, training, and readiness.
column14: NC Senate, HB 589, July 27, 2017, https://www.ncleg.gov/Sessions/2017/Bills/House/PDF/H589v5.pdf.

column13: In May 2019, Oklahoma Governor Kevin Sitt signed House Bill 2118 which establishes setback requirements for the construction of individual wind turbines construction near military airspace, including military training routes, runway approaches, drop zones and bombing ranges. The law requires developers to submit to the Corporation Commission and the Oklahoma Aeronautics Commission a “Determination of No Hazard” from the Federal Aviation Administration and documentation showing that any adverse impacts to the Department of Defense have been resolved. Two weeks after the passage of the bill, NextEra Energy cancelled two proposed wind projects: Minco V and Crowder.
column14: Oklahoma H.B. 2118 (2019), http://www.oklegislature.gov/BillInfo.aspx?Bill=hb2118&Session=1900; Katelyn Howard, NextEra Energy Cancels Plans for Two Wind Power Projects, KGOU, May 20, 2019, https://www.kgou.org/business-and-economy/2019-05-29/nextera-energy-cancels-plans-for-two-wind-power-projects; James Neal, Legislature passes new bill to protect military airspace from wind turbine encroachment, Enid News And Eagle, May 1, 2019, https://www.enidnews.com/oklahoma/news/legislature-passes-new-bill-to-protect-military-airspace-from-wind-turbine-encroachment/article_9336b11c-5fb0-5ba0-a58d-304839c7bc0e.html.

column13: Under House Bill 291, adopted in 2014, wind turbines must be set back at least 1,000 feet from property lines and at least 2,000 feet from “any residential neighborhood, school, hospital, or nursing home facility.”
column14: Ky. Rev. Stat. Ann. § 278.704(2) (2014), https://apps.legislature.ky.gov/law/statutes/statute.aspx?id=54023.

column13: A Minnesota administrative rule provides that “[n]o large electric power generating plant site may be permitted where the developed portion of the plant site, excluding water storage reservoirs and cooling ponds, includes more than 0.5 acres of prime farmland per megawatt of net generating capacity, unless there is no feasible and prudent alternative.”
column14: Minn. R. 7850.4400(4), https://www.revisor.mn.gov/rules/7850.4400/.

column13: H.B. 483 requires that wind turbines be sited at least 1,125 feet from the tip of the turbine’s nearest blade to the nearest adjacent property line.
column14: Ohio H.B. 483 (effective June 16, 2014), http://archives.legislature.state.oh.us/bills.cfm?ID=130_HB_483; Kathiann M. Kowalsi, Industry: Setback changes will end new wind farms in Ohio, Energy News Network, June 19, 2014, https://energynews.us/2014/06/19/industry-setback-changes-will-end-new-wind-farms-in-ohio/.

column13: S.B. 52 which took effect October 11, 2021, gives counties the power to veto specific projects and to declare themselves categorically off-limits to large renewable energy projects. Specifically, the law allows counties to adopt binding resolutions to (a) prohibit or limit the construction of a proposed project; and/or (b) designate a “restricted area” in which wind projects of at least 5 MW and solar projects of at least 50 MW are prohibited or otherwise limited. Renewable energy developers must also receive project approval from the Ohio Power Siting Board (OPSB), giving opponents a second opportunity to contest projects if they do not succeed in lobbying the county government for a resolution.
column14: Jeffery Tomich, ‘Volatile place.’ New laws thwart Ohio renewables, Energy Wire, Aug 5, 2021, https://www.eenews.net/articles/volatile-place-new-laws-thwart-ohio-renewables; Ohio S.B. 52 (effective Oct. 11, 2021), https://www.legislature.ohio.gov/legislation/134/sb52.

column13: Since August 21, 2015, Oklahoma has required that wind energy facilities be set back 1.5 miles from any public school, hospital, or airport.
column14: Okla. Stat. § 17-160.20(A), https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/2022/title-17/section-17-160-20/.

column13: In April 2018, the Pennsylvania Game Commission unanimously approved a wind energy moratorium across over 1.5 million acres of game lands in the state. The Commission stated that it recognizes the value in renewable energy systems, but that the construction and operation of wind energy would threaten the habitat stability of game and other species in the area. The Commission therefore “declare[d] as policy that wind energy development on State Game Lands [is] inconsistent with the responsibilities of the Pennsylvania Game Commission under both the Game and Wildlife Code and Article I, Section 27 of the Pennsylvania Constitution.”
column14: Commonwealth Of Pennsylvania, Pennsylvania Game Commission, Resolution of the Board of Commissioners, April 24, 2018, https://www.pgc.pa.gov/InformationResources/AgencyBusinessCenter/WindEnergy/Documents/Resolution%20Establishing%20Windmill%20Policy.pdf; BCSCL Staff, Board enacts moratorium on game lands wind energy projects, Beaver County Sportsmen’s Conservation League, June 4, 2018, https://bcscl.net/pa-game-commission/board-enacts-moratorium-on-game-lands-wind-energy-projects/.

column13: On May 3, 2023, the state adopted a law directing the New York Power Authority (NYPA) to develop renewable energy projects “to support the state’s renewable energy goals established pursuant to the climate leadership and community protection act.” While the law generally encourages renewable energy development, it places two significant limitations on where renewable energy facilities may be sited. First, the law specifies that NYPA shall not develop renewable energy generation projects on “land used in agricultural production . . . unless [the] project is in furtherance of an agrivoltaics project” (i.e., unless the project is designed to accommodate dual-use farming and energy production). Second, the law provides that NYPA “shall . . . not build on lands located upon any Native American territory or reservation . . . except through voluntary sale or other agreement for such use with the consent of the relevant nation.”
column14: 2023 N.Y. Laws, ch. 56, part QQ, sec. 1 (to be codified N.Y. Pub. Auth. Law. § 1005(27-a)(b)).

column13: In 2017, the Legislature enacted Public Act No. 17-218, which prohibits solar photovoltaic projects of 2 MW or greater on “core forest” or “prime farmland” statewide unless state regulators find that the project will not “materially affect” the land’s status as core forest or prime farmland.
column14: CT Gen Stat § 16-50k (rev. Jan. 1, 2021), https://www.cga.ct.gov/current/pub/chap_277a.htm#sec_16-50k; id. § 16a-3k (rev. Jan. 1, 2021), https://www.cga.ct.gov/current/pub/chap_295.htm#sec_16a-3k.

column13: In June 2023, Senate Bill 1127 was signed into law by Gov. JB Pritzker after passing through the House and Senate without opposition. SB 1127 prevents future solar and wind development in the Lee-Ogle Enterprise Zone, a 2,000-acre area in and southeast of Rochelle.
column14: Jeff Helfrich, Legislation protecting local enterprise zone area signed into law, Rochelle News-Leader, June 20, 2023, https://rochellenews-leader.com/article/legislation-protecting-local-enterprise-zone-area-signed-into-law.

column13: S.B. 583, codified at W.V. Code §24-2-1o, limits utility-scale solar generation capacity in several ways. The law prohibits any single solar generation facility from having a generating capacity greater than 200 MW, and the law sets a cumulative generating capacity limit of 400 MW for all solar generation facilities “operating at any given time, and for which rate recovery is provided by the commission under this section.” The law also prohibits solar generation facilities from “hav[ing] a generating capacity greater than 50 megawatts until such time as 85 percent of that . . . facility’s annual energy output is being sold or is contracted to be sold to residential, commercial, or industrial customers pursuant to a renewable special contract or renewable tariff.” After that point, the facility’s generating capacity can expand in increments no greater than 50 MW until the facility again reaches the 85% benchmark. S.B. 583, codified at W.V. Code §24-2-1o, limits utility-scale solar generation capacity in several ways. The law prohibits any single solar generation facility from having a generating capacity greater than 200 MW, and the law sets a cumulative generating capacity limit of 400 MW for all solar generation facilities “operating at any given time, and for which rate recovery is provided by the commission under this section.” The law also prohibits solar generation facilities from “hav[ing] a generating capacity greater than 50 megawatts until such time as 85 percent of that . . . facility’s annual energy output is being sold or is contracted to be sold to residential, commercial, or industrial customers pursuant to a renewable special contract or renewable tariff.” After that point, the facility’s generating capacity can expand in increments no greater than 50 MW until the facility again reaches the 85% benchmark.

column13: In 2004, Kansas Governor Kathleen Sebelius instated a wind development moratorium on parts of the Flint Hills in order to protect the tallgrass prairie. In 2011, Governor Sam Brownback doubled the protected area, renaming it the “Tallgrass Heartland.” This moratorium, which covers parts of 12 counties, was continued in July 2020 by Governor Laura Kelly.
column14: State of Kansas Proclamation by the Governor, July 28, 2020, https://governor.kansas.gov/wp-content/uploads/2020/07/20200728140630901.pdf. Chad Frey, Kelly reaffirms wind moratorium, The Kansan, July 30, 2020, https://www.patriotledger.com/story/news/2020/07/30/kelly-reaffirms-wind-moratorium/114905584/.

column13: On October 23, 2024, Wibaux County became the first county in Montana to adopt countywide zoning to restrict wind development. The new zoning rules require wind turbines to be set back 1,250 from property lines and 1.5 miles from residences. The rules were adopted as part of an effort to block AES’s proposal to construct the 235-MW Wibaux Wind Farm, which would include 50 MW of battery storage.
column14: Karin Kirk, Property Rights Take Center Stage as Montana Grapples with Wind Development, Inside Climate News, May 14, 2025, https://insideclimatenews.org/news/14052025/montana-wind-development-property-rights/; Peyton Olson, Rural County Adopts Zoning…Against Wind Energy, MEIC, Dec. 10, 2024, https://meic.org/rural-county-adopts-zoning-against-wind-energy/.

column13: In May 2025, the Carroll County Quorum Court passed a 5-year moratorium banning any new commercial wind or solar energy facilities in the county.
column14: Bill Bowden, Carroll County bans wind farms for five years, ARKANSAS DEMOCRAT GAZETTE, May 22, 2025, https://www.arkansasonline.com/news/2025/may/22/carroll-county-bans-wind-farms-for-5-years/.

column13: In December 2024, the Town of Lyme adopted a 12-month moratorium on all solar energy systems and/or battery energy storage systems.

column13: On March 26, 2025, the Caldwell County Fiscal Court unanimously adopted Ordinance 03-15-2024-02,establishing licensing requirements for large scale solar energy systems, including rooftop solar and ground-mounted solar. The requirements include 2,000-foot setbacks from any non-participating property and decommissioning plans to be updated at least every 5 years. Violation of the ordinance constitutes a misdemeanor offense, carrying a fine of up to $500.00.

column13: On April 29, 2024, the Lyon County Fiscal Court unanimously adopted Ordinance 04-2024 establishing licensing requirements for large scale solar energy systems, including rooftop solar and ground-mounted solar. The requirements include 2,000-foot setbacks from any non-participating property and decommissioning plans to be updated at least every 5 years. The ordinance provides that a violation constitutes a misdemeanor offense, carrying a fine of up to $500.00.
column14: Lyon County, Ky., Ordinance 04-2024, April 29, 2024, https://climate.law.columbia.edu/sites/climate.law.columbia.edu/files/content/Lyon%20County%20Ordinance%2004-2024%2C%204.29.24%20(1).pdf

column13: In March 2024, the Kiskiminetas Township Planning Commission began discussions to revise the Township’s Commercial Solar Energy Systems (CSES) Ordinance. On July 9, 2025, the Board of Supervisors approved the new ordinance. The new ordinance imposes a $50,000 annual development fee per 1-MW of nameplate capacity for large-scale solar projects, as well as a non-refundable $2,500 application fee. The ordinance also limits solar development to industrial zoning districts as a conditional use; and establishes 300-foot setbacks from property lines and 10-foot height restrictions.

column13: On July 17, 2025, the Richland Board of County Commissioners adoption a resolution to ban on utility-scale wind and solar development in 11 of the county’s 18 townships. Local residents formed a group called Richland County Citizens for Property Rights and Job Development, which is working to get the ban reversed. The group circulated a petition calling for a referendum on the Commissioners’ resolution. The petition garnered 3,380 signatures, enough for the issue to be put on to voters at the next primary elections in May 2026.

Now, residents are pushing back., CANARY MEDIA, Nov. 18, 2025, https://www.canarymedia.com/articles/clean-energy/richland-ohio-wind-solar-ban-vote;  Richland County Board of Commissioners, Meeting Minutes, July 17, 2025, https://www.richlandcountyoh.gov/media/Departments/Commissioners/Documents/Meeting%20Minutes%20-%202025/55.%20July%2017,%202025.pdf.

column13: In May 2023, the Clinton County Board of Commissioners adopted Resolution 23-510 declaring all unincorporated areas of the county as restricted areas in which construction of utility-scale wind and solar power generation facilities are prohibited, per Senate Bill 52.

column13:
In April 2025, the Arkansas legislature enacted the Wind Energy Development Act (SB437) which was signed into law on April 22, 2025. The law became effective on August 3, 2025, though its implementing rules (adopted on January 14, 2026) are still pending Legislative Council approval.

The law  created Arkansas’ first statewide permitting regime for wind energy facilities of at least 5 megawatts (MW) and exceeding 200 feet in height. Among other things, the law prohibits wind energy facilities from causing interference to radio, television, or internet reception on adjoining properties; requires developers to file insurance policies, acoustics expert reports, and financial security documentation at least 30 days before commencing construction; and requires developers to publish notice of the project at least once per week, for two consecutive weeks, within 30 days before filing the permit application.
column14: Ark. Code Ann. § 23-18-1401 et seq. (Act 945, 2025), https://arkleg.state.ar.us/Bills/Detail.

column13:
On May 1, 2025, the St. Clair County Health Department adopted Regulation 25-01, titled the “St. Clair County Health Department’s Regulation Regarding Solar Energy and Battery Energy Storage Facilities.” The regulation was also approved by the St. Clair County Board of Commissioners on May 1, 2025. The regulation establishes a new approval process, administered by the Health Department, for “industrial” solar and battery energy storage facilities. The regulation also limits permissible noise levels at solar and battery energy storage facilities, imposes standards regarding the facilities’ visual impacts, and mandates financial security sufficient to fully remove all subsurface improvements associated with the facilities at the time of decommissioning. Violations of these requirements are classified as misdemeanor offenses subject to enforcement by the Health Department.

In July 2025, DTE Electric Company, the developer of large-scale solar and battery storage facilities in St. Clair County, filed a lawsuit in the St. Clair County Circuit Court challenging the regulation. In February 2026, the court overturned the regulation, ruling that it was in fact a zoning law attempting to regulate solar development and the county had overstepped its authority. In late February 2026, the St. Clair County Board of Commissioners voted to appeal the court’s ruling.
